**Trotskyism** ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Троцкизм, *Trotskizm*) is the political ideology and branch of [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism") and [Leninism](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism") developed by Russian revolutionary and [intellectual](/wiki/Intellectual "Intellectual")[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") along with some other members of the [Left Opposition](/wiki/Left_Opposition "Left Opposition") and the [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International"). Trotsky described himself as an [orthodox Marxist](/wiki/Orthodox_Marxist "Orthodox Marxist"), a [revolutionary Marxist](/wiki/Revolutionary_socialism "Revolutionary socialism"), and a [Bolshevik](/wiki/Bolshevik "Bolshevik")–[Leninist](/wiki/Leninist "Leninist") as well as a follower of [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx"), [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels"), [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin"), [Karl Liebknecht](/wiki/Karl_Liebknecht "Karl Liebknecht"), and [Rosa Luxemburg](/wiki/Rosa_Luxemburg "Rosa Luxemburg"). His relations with Lenin have been a source of intense historical debate.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwain20141–10-3)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorth2010[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidmVqvouA22IkCqdavidnorthindefenceoftrotsky_52–90]-4) However, on balance, scholarly opinion among a range of prominent [historians](/wiki/Historians "Historians") and [political scientists](/wiki/Political_scientists "Political scientists") such as [E. H. Carr](/wiki/E._H._Carr "E. H. Carr"), [Isaac Deutscher](/wiki/Isaac_Deutscher "Isaac Deutscher"), [Moshe Lewin](/wiki/Moshe_Lewin "Moshe Lewin"), [Ronald Suny](/wiki/Ronald_Suny "Ronald Suny"), Richard B. Day and [W. Bruce Lincoln](/wiki/W._Bruce_Lincoln "W. Bruce Lincoln") was that Lenin's desired "heir" would have been a [collective responsibility](/wiki/Collective_leadership "Collective leadership") in which Trotsky was placed in "an important role and within which [Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") would be dramatically demoted (if not removed)".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Trotsky advocated for a [decentralized](/wiki/Decentralization "Decentralization") form of [economic planning](/wiki/Economic_planning "Economic planning"),[[6]](#cite_note-BRILL-6) [workers' control](/wiki/Workers%27_control "Workers' control") of production,[[7]](#cite_note-books.google.com-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) elected representation of Soviet [socialist parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_Soviet_Union "List of political parties in the Soviet Union"),[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015293-9)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotsky1991218-10) mass [soviet](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)") [democratization](/wiki/Democratization "Democratization"),[[11]](#cite_note-Taylor_&_Francis-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978207–215-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)
the tactic of a [united front](/wiki/United_front "United front") against [far-right](/wiki/Far-right "Far-right") parties,[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETicktin1992227-14)
[cultural](/wiki/Cultural "Cultural") autonomy for artistic movements,[[15]](#cite_note-Marxism_and_Literary_Criticism-15) voluntary [collectivisation](/wiki/Collectivisation "Collectivisation"),[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeilharz1987[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidLfe-DwAAQBAJdqtrotskywidelyacknowledgedcollectivisationpgPT196_196–197]-16)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERubenstein2011[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleontrotskyrevol0000rubepage160mode2upqforcedcollectivization_161]-17) a [transitional program](/wiki/The_Death_Agony_of_Capitalism_and_the_Tasks_of_the_Fourth_International "The Death Agony of Capitalism and the Tasks of the Fourth International"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) and socialist [internationalism](/wiki/Proletarian_internationalism "Proletarian internationalism").[[19]](#cite_note-19) He supported founding a [vanguard party](/wiki/Vanguard_party "Vanguard party") of the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat"), and a [dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat "Dictatorship of the proletariat") (as opposed to the "[dictatorship of the bourgeoisie](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_bourgeoisie "Dictatorship of the bourgeoisie")", which Marxists argue is a major component of [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism")) based on working-class self-emancipation and [council democracy](/wiki/Council_democracy "Council democracy"). Trotsky also adhered to [scientific socialism](/wiki/Scientific_socialism "Scientific socialism") and viewed this as a conscious expression of historical processes.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotsky2019138-20) Trotskyists are [critical](/wiki/Anti-Stalinist_left "Anti-Stalinist left") of [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") as they oppose Stalin's theory of [socialism in one country](/wiki/Socialism_in_one_country "Socialism in one country") in favour of Trotsky's theory of [permanent revolution](/wiki/Permanent_revolution "Permanent revolution"). Trotskyists criticize the [bureaucracy](/wiki/Bureaucracy "Bureaucracy") and anti-democratic current developed in the [Soviet Union under Stalin](/wiki/Soviet_Union_under_Stalin "Soviet Union under Stalin").

Despite their ideological disputes, Trotsky and Lenin were close personally prior to the [London Congress of Social Democrats](/wiki/2nd_Congress_of_the_Russian_Social_Democratic_Labour_Party "2nd Congress of the Russian Social Democratic Labour Party") in 1903 and during the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"). Lenin and Trotsky were close ideologically and personally during the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") and its aftermath. Trotskyists and some others call Trotsky its "co-leader".[[21]](#cite_note-21) This was also alluded to by Rosa Luxemburg.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Lenin himself never mentioned the concept of "Trotskyism" after Trotsky became a member of the Bolshevik party.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogovin2021281-23) Trotsky was the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army")'s paramount leader in the Revolutionary period's direct aftermath. Trotsky initially opposed some aspects of [Leninism](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism"),[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) but eventually concluded that unity between the [Mensheviks](/wiki/Mensheviks "Mensheviks") and [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") was impossible and joined the Bolsheviks. Trotsky played a leading role with Lenin in the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution"). Lenin and Trotsky were also both honorary presidents of the [Third International](/wiki/Communist_International "Communist International").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015605-26) Trotskyists have traditionally drawn upon [Lenin's testament](/wiki/Lenin%27s_testament "Lenin's testament") and his alliance with Trotsky in 1922–23 against the [Soviet bureaucracy](/wiki/Soviet_bureaucracy "Soviet bureaucracy") as primary evidence that Lenin sought to remove Stalin from the position of [General Secretary](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union").[[27]](#cite_note-27) Various historians have also cited Lenin's proposal to appoint Trotsky [Vice-Chairman of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Deputy_Premier_of_the_Soviet_Union "Deputy Premier of the Soviet Union") as further evidence that he intended Trotsky to be his successor as [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In October 1927, by order of Stalin,[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30)[*[unreliable source](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] Trotsky was removed from power. In November of the same year, he was expelled from the [All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks)](/wiki/All-Union_Communist_Party_(Bolsheviks) "All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks)") (VKP(b)). He was exiled to [Alma-Ata](/wiki/Almaty "Almaty") (now Almaty) in January 1928 and then expelled from the [USSR](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") in February 1929. As the head of the [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International"), Trotsky continued in exile to oppose what he termed the [degenerated workers' state](/wiki/Degenerated_workers%27_state "Degenerated workers' state") in the USSR. On 20 August 1940, Trotsky was attacked in [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") by [Ramón Mercader](/wiki/Ram%C3%B3n_Mercader "Ramón Mercader"), a Spanish-born [NKVD](/wiki/NKVD "NKVD") agent, and died the next day in a hospital. His murder is considered a political assassination. Almost all Trotskyists within the VKP(b) had been executed in the [Great Purges](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge") of 1937–1938, effectively removing all of Trotsky's internal influence in the USSR. [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") had come to power as head of the Communist Party in Ukraine, signing lists of other Trotskyists to be executed. Trotsky and the party of Trotskyists were still recognized as enemies of the USSR during Khrushchev's rule of the USSR after 1956.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Trotsky's [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International") was established in the [French Third Republic](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") in 1938 when Trotskyists argued that the [Comintern](/wiki/Comintern "Comintern") or Third International had become irretrievably "lost to Stalinism" and thus incapable of leading the international working class to political power.[[32]](#cite_note-transitional-32)

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

According to Trotsky, his programme could be distinguished from other Marxist theories by five key elements:

* Support for the strategy of [permanent revolution](/wiki/Permanent_revolution "Permanent revolution") in opposition to the [two-stage theory](/wiki/Two-stage_theory "Two-stage theory") of his opponents.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[34]](#cite_note-34)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]
* Criticism of the post-1924 leadership of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics and analysis of its features,[[35]](#cite_note-35) after 1933, also support for [political revolution](/wiki/Political_revolution_(Trotskyism) "Political revolution (Trotskyism)") in the USSR and what Trotskyists term the [degenerated workers' states](/wiki/Degenerated_workers%27_state "Degenerated workers' state").
* Support for [social revolution](/wiki/Social_revolution "Social revolution") in the advanced capitalist countries through working-class mass action.
* Support for [proletarian internationalism](/wiki/Proletarian_internationalism "Proletarian internationalism").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]
* Use of a transitional programme of demands that bridge between daily struggles of the working class and the maximal ideas of the socialist transformation of society.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

On the [political spectrum](/wiki/Political_spectrum "Political spectrum") of [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism"), Trotskyists are usually considered to be on the left. In the 1920s, they called themselves the [Left Opposition](/wiki/Left_Opposition "Left Opposition"), although today's [left communism](/wiki/Left_communism "Left communism") is distinct and usually non-Bolshevik. The terminological disagreement can be confusing because different versions of a [left-right political spectrum](/wiki/Left%E2%80%93right_politics "Left–right politics") are used. [Anti-revisionists](/wiki/Anti-Revisionism "Anti-Revisionism") consider themselves the ultimate leftists on a spectrum from communism on the left to imperialist capitalism on the right. However, given that [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") is often labelled rightist within the communist spectrum and [left communism](/wiki/Left_communism "Left communism") leftist, anti-revisionists' idea of the left is very different from that of left communism. Despite being Bolshevik-Leninist comrades during the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") and [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War"), Trotsky and Stalin became enemies in the 1920s and, after that, opposed the legitimacy of each other's forms of Leninism. Trotsky was highly [critical of the Stalinist USSR](/wiki/Anti-Stalinism "Anti-Stalinism") for suppressing democracy and the lack of adequate economic planning.[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30)

Overall, Trotsky and the Left-United Opposition factions advocated for rapid [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization"), voluntary collectivisation of agriculture, and the expansion of a [worker's democracy](/wiki/Soviet_democracy "Soviet democracy").[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Main article: [Permanent revolution](/wiki/Permanent_revolution "Permanent revolution")

Until 1905, some revolutionaries[[40]](#cite_note-40) claimed that [Marx's theory of history](/wiki/Marx%27s_theory_of_history "Marx's theory of history") posited that only a revolution in a European capitalist society would lead to a socialist one. According to this position, a socialist revolution could not occur in a backward, feudal country such as early 20th-century Russia when it had such a small and almost powerless capitalist class. In 1905, Trotsky formulated his theory of [permanent revolution](/wiki/Permanent_revolution "Permanent revolution"), which later became a defining characteristic of Trotskyism.

The theory of permanent revolution addressed how such feudal regimes were to be overthrown and how socialism could establish itself, given the lack of economic prerequisites. Trotsky argued that only the working class could overthrow feudalism and win the [peasantry](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant")'s support in Russia. Furthermore, he argued that the Russian working class would not stop there. They would win their revolution against the weak capitalist class, establish a workers' state in Russia, and appeal to the working class in the advanced capitalist countries worldwide. As a result, the global working class would come to Russia's aid, and socialism could develop worldwide.

According to political scientist Baruch Knei-Paz, Trotsky's theory of “permanent revolution” was grossly misrepresented by [Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") as [defeatist](/wiki/Defeatism "Defeatism") and adventurist during the succession struggle, when in fact Trotsky encouraged revolutions in Europe but was not at any time proposing “reckless confrontations” with the capitalist world.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978343-41)

### Capitalist or bourgeois-democratic revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Capitalist or bourgeois-democratic revolution")]

Revolutions in Britain in the 17th century and in France in 1789 abolished feudalism and established the essential requisites for the development of capitalism. Trotsky argued that these revolutions would not be repeated in Russia.

In *Results and Prospects*, written in 1906, Trotsky outlines his theory in detail, arguing: "History does not repeat itself. However much one may compare the Russian Revolution with the Great French Revolution, the former can never be transformed into a repetition of the latter."[[42]](#cite_note-42) In the [French Revolution of 1789](/wiki/French_Revolution_of_1789 "French Revolution of 1789"), France experienced what Marxists called a "[bourgeois-democratic revolution](/wiki/Bourgeois-democratic_revolution "Bourgeois-democratic revolution")"—a regime was established wherein the bourgeoisie overthrew the existing French feudalistic system. The bourgeoisie then moved towards establishing a regime of democratic parliamentary institutions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, while democratic rights were extended to the bourgeoisie, they were not generally extended to a universal franchise. The freedom for workers to organize unions or to strike was not achieved without considerable struggle.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Passivity of the bourgeoisie

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Passivity of the bourgeoisie")]

Trotsky argues that countries like Russia had no "enlightened, active" revolutionary [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") which could play the same role, and the working class constituted a tiny minority. In his book, Results and Prospects, Trotsky states that, by the time of the European revolutions of 1848, "the bourgeoisie was already unable to play a comparable role. It did not want and was not able to undertake the revolutionary liquidation of the social system that stood in its path to power."[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The theory of permanent revolution considers that in many countries that are thought, by Trotskyists, to not have completed a [bourgeois-democratic revolution](/wiki/Bourgeois_revolution "Bourgeois revolution"), the capitalist class opposes the creation of any revolutionary situation, as a revolutionary proletariat may threaten their position in society. Trotsky believed that, "[the bourgeoisie] fear of the armed proletariat is greater than the fear of the soldiery of the autocracy."[[44]](#cite_note-44) In the case of Russia, the working class, although a small minority in a predominantly peasant-based society, was organised in vast factories, owned by the capitalist class, and into large working-class districts. Proletariat organisation meant that, during the Russian Revolution of 1905, the bourgeoisie found it necessary to ally with reactionary elements, such as the essentially feudal landlords and, ultimately, the existing Czarist Russian state forces. This was to protect capitalist class ownership of property, such as factories, banks, etc.—from expropriation by the revolutionary proletariat.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Therefore, according to the theory of permanent revolution, the capitalist classes of economically backward countries are incapable of carrying through revolutionary change. As a result, they are linked to and rely on the feudal landowners in many ways.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Thus, Trotsky argues that because a majority of the branches of industry in Russia originated under the direct influence of government measures—sometimes with the help of government subsidies—the capitalist class was again tied to the ruling elite. The capitalist class was subservient to European capital.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### The incapability of the peasantry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The incapability of the peasantry")]

The theory of permanent revolution further considers that the [peasantry](/wiki/Peasantry "Peasantry") as a whole cannot take on the task of carrying through the revolution because it is dispersed in small holdings throughout the country and forms a heterogeneous grouping, including the rich peasants who employ rural workers and aspire to [landlordism](/wiki/Landlord "Landlord") as well as the poor peasants who aspire to own more land. Trotsky argues: "All historical experience [...] shows that the peasantry are absolutely incapable of taking up an independent political role".[[47]](#cite_note-Results_204–205-47)

### The key role of the proletariat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: The key role of the proletariat")]

Trotskyists differ on the extent to which this is true today. However, even the most orthodox tend to recognise in the late twentieth century a new development in the revolts of the rural poor: the self-organising struggles of the landless, along with many other struggles that in some ways reflect the militant united, organised struggles of the working class, which to various degrees do not bear the marks of class divisions typical of the heroic peasant struggles of previous epochs. However, orthodox Trotskyists today still argue that the town- and city-based working-class struggle is central to the task of a successful socialist revolution linked to these struggles of the rural poor. They argue that the working class learns of the necessity to conduct a collective struggle, for instance, in trade unions, arising from its social conditions in the factories and workplaces; and that the collective consciousness it achieves is an essential ingredient of the socialist reconstruction of society.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Trotsky himself argued that only the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat") or working class was capable of achieving the tasks of that bourgeois revolution. In 1905, the working class in Russia, a generation brought together in vast factories from the relative isolation of peasant life, saw the result of its labour as a vast collective effort, also seeing the only means of struggling against its oppression in terms of a collective effort, forming workers councils ([soviets](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)")) in the course of the revolution of that year. In 1906, Trotsky argued:

> The factory system brings the proletariat to the foreground [...] The proletariat immediately found itself concentrated in tremendous masses, while between these masses and the autocracy there stood a capitalist bourgeoisie, very small in numbers, isolated from the "people", half-foreign, without historical traditions, and inspired only by the greed for gain.

— Leon Trotsky, *Results and Prospects*[[49]](#cite_note-49)

For instance, the [Putilov Factory](/wiki/Kirov_Plant "Kirov Plant") numbered 12,000 workers in 1900 and, according to Trotsky, 36,000 in July 1917.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Although only a tiny minority in Russian society, the proletariat would lead a revolution to emancipate the peasantry and thus "secure the support of the peasantry" as part of that revolution, on whose support it would rely.[[47]](#cite_note-Results_204–205-47)[[note 1]](#cite_note-51) However, to improve their conditions, the working class must create a revolution of their own, which would accomplish the bourgeois revolution and establish a workers' state.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### International revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: International revolution")]

Only fully developed capitalist conditions prepare the basis for socialism. According to [classical Marxism](/wiki/Classical_Marxism "Classical Marxism"), a revolution in peasant-based countries such as Russia ultimately prepares the ground for capitalism's development since the liberated peasants become small owners, producers, and traders. This leads to the growth of commodity markets, from which a new capitalist class emerges.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Trotsky agreed that a new socialist state and economy in a country like Russia would not be able to hold out against the pressures of a hostile capitalist world and the internal pressures of its backward economy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Trotsky argued that the revolution must quickly spread to capitalist countries, bringing about a socialist revolution that must spread worldwide. In this way, the revolution is "permanent", moving out of necessity first, from the bourgeois revolution to the workers' revolution and from there, uninterruptedly, to European and worldwide revolutions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

An [internationalist](/wiki/Proletarian_internationalism "Proletarian internationalism") outlook of permanent revolution is found in the works of [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx"). The term "permanent revolution" is taken from a remark of Marx in his March 1850 Address: "it is our task", Marx said:

> [...] to make the revolution permanent until all the more or less propertied classes have been driven from their ruling positions, until the proletariat has conquered state power and until the association of the proletarians has progressed sufficiently far—not only in one country but in all the leading countries of the world—that competition between the proletarians of these countries ceases and at least the decisive forces of production are concentrated in the hands of the workers.

— Karl Marx, *Address of the Central Committee to the Communist League*[[51]](#cite_note-52)

His biographer, Isaac Deutscher, has explicitly contrasted his support for proletarian internationalism against his opposition to revolution by [military](/wiki/Military "Military") [conquest](/wiki/Conquest "Conquest") as seen with his documented opposition to the [war with Poland](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Soviet_War "Polish–Soviet War") in 1920, proposed armistice with the [Entente](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") and temperance with staging [anti-British revolts](/wiki/British_foreign_policy_in_the_Middle_East "British foreign policy in the Middle East") in the Middle East.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015472–473-53) Historian [Robert Vincent Daniels](/wiki/Robert_Vincent_Daniels "Robert Vincent Daniels") believed that the practical differences, in the domain of [international policy](/wiki/Foreign_policy "Foreign policy"), between the Left Opposition and other factions had been exaggerated and he contended that Trotsky was no more prepared than other Bolshevik figures to risk [war](/wiki/War "War") or for the loss of [trade](/wiki/Trade "Trade") opportunities despite his support for [world revolution](/wiki/World_revolution "World revolution").[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels2008195-54)

### Socialist democracy and Workers' Control

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Socialist democracy and Workers' Control")]

Main articles: [Socialist democracy](/wiki/Socialist_democracy "Socialist democracy"), [Union democracy](/wiki/Union_democracy "Union democracy"), [Workers Control](/wiki/Workers_Control "Workers Control"), [Our Political Tasks](/wiki/Our_Political_Tasks "Our Political Tasks"), [New Course (Trotsky book)](/wiki/New_Course_(Trotsky_book) "New Course (Trotsky book)"), and [Soviet democracy](/wiki/Soviet_democracy "Soviet democracy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Lev_Trotsky_1906-3.3_V1.jpg/250px-Lev_Trotsky_1906-3.3_V1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lev_Trotsky_1906-3.3_V1.jpg)

The [Soviet of Workers' Deputies of St. Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Soviet "Saint Petersburg Soviet") in 1905, Trotsky in the center. The [soviets](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)") were an early example of a [workers council](/wiki/Workers_council "Workers council").

Prior to the October Revolution, Trotsky had been part of a tendency toward [radical democracy](/wiki/Radical_democracy "Radical democracy") which included both Left [Mensheviks](/wiki/Mensheviks "Mensheviks") and Left [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels2008181-55) His work, *[Our Political Tasks](/wiki/Our_Political_Tasks "Our Political Tasks")*, published in 1904 reviewed issues related to party organisation, [mass participation](/wiki/Public_participation "Public participation") and the potential dangers of [substitutionism](/wiki/Substitutionism "Substitutionism") which he foresaw in a Leninist party model.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978176–199-56) Trotsky would also assume a central role in the [1905 revolution](/wiki/1905_revolution "1905 revolution")[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58) and serve as the Chairman of the Petersburg Soviet of Workers' Delegates in which he wrote several proclamations urging for improved [economic conditions](/wiki/Labour_rights "Labour rights"), [political rights](/wiki/Political_rights "Political rights") and the use of [strike action](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action") against the [Tsarist regime](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") on behalf of workers.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThatcher2005[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidcU3yFMLm1voCdqtrotsky1905stpetersburgsovietpgPT39_38–40]-59)

In 1917, he had proposed the election of a new Soviet [presidium](/wiki/Presidium "Presidium") with other [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") parties based on [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation").[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015293-9) On the other hand, he had accepted the ban on rival parties in Moscow during the Russian Civil War due to their opposition to the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution"). Yet, he also opposed the extension of the ban to the Mensheviks in [Soviet Georgia](/wiki/Georgian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Georgian Soviet Socialist Republic").[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015595-60)

In 1922, Lenin allied with [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") against the party's growing [bureaucratisation](/wiki/Nomenklatura "Nomenklatura") and the influence of [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin").[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65) In 1923, Trotsky and a number of [Old Bolsheviks](/wiki/Old_Bolsheviks "Old Bolsheviks") who signed [The Declaration of 46](/wiki/The_Declaration_of_46 "The Declaration of 46") raised concerns to the Politburo concerning intra-party democracy which shared similarities with Lenin's [proposed party reforms](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin#Declining_health_and_conflict_with_Stalin:_1920–1923 "Vladimir Lenin") before his death. The signatories of the 46 letter expressed grievances related to the provincial conferences, party congresses, and the election of committees. Separately, Trotsky would develop his views further with the publication of the *[New Course](/wiki/New_Course_(Trotsky_book) "New Course (Trotsky book)")* in 1924.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogovin2021155–182-66)

In 1927, Trotsky and the [United Opposition](/wiki/United_Opposition_(Soviet_Union) "United Opposition (Soviet Union)") had argued for the expansion of [industrial democracy](/wiki/Economic_democracy "Economic democracy") with their joint platform which demanded majority representation of workers in trade union congresses including the [All-Union Congress](/wiki/All-Russian_Congress_of_Soviets "All-Russian Congress of Soviets") and an increase of non-party workers to one-third of representation in these elected organs.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogovin2021418–419-67) They also supported legal protection for workers' right to criticise, such as the right to make independent proposals. According to historian [Vadim Rogovin](/wiki/Vadim_Rogovin "Vadim Rogovin"), these proposals would have developed democracy in the sphere of [production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production") and facilitated the establishment of workers' control over economic management.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogovin2021418–419-67)

Following Stalin's consolidation of power in the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and static centralization of political power, Trotsky condemned the Soviet government's policies for lacking widespread democratic participation on the part of the population and for suppressing [workers' self-management](/wiki/Workers%27_self-management "Workers' self-management") and democratic participation in the management of the economy. Because these authoritarian political measures were inconsistent with the organizational precepts of socialism, Trotsky characterized the Soviet Union as a deformed workers' state that could not effectively transition to socialism. Ostensibly socialist states where democracy is lacking, yet the economy is largely in the hands of the state, are termed by [orthodox Trotskyist](/wiki/Orthodox_Trotskyism "Orthodox Trotskyism") theories as [degenerated](/wiki/Degenerated_workers%27_state "Degenerated workers' state") or [deformed](/wiki/Deformed_workers%27_state "Deformed workers' state") workers' states and not socialist states.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

> Bureaucratic autocracy must give place to Soviet democracy. A restoration of the right of criticism, and a genuine freedom of elections, are necessary conditions for the further development of the country. This assumes a revival of freedom of Soviet parties, beginning with the party of Bolsheviks, and a resurrection of the trade unions. The bringing of democracy into industry means a radical revision of plans in the interests of toilers.

Trotsky, *The Revolution Betrayed*, 1936[[68]](#cite_note-69)

In 1931, Trotsky [wrote](/wiki/The_Spanish_Revolution,_1931%E2%80%931939_(Trotsky_book) "The Spanish Revolution, 1931–1939 (Trotsky book)") a pamphlet on the [Spanish Revolution](/wiki/Spanish_Revolution_of_1936 "Spanish Revolution of 1936") and called for the creation of worker's juntas in emulation of [elected soviets](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)") as an expression of [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") organisation.[[69]](#cite_note-70) Trotsky stipulated the need for shared participation of the communist factions, [anarcho-syndicalists](/wiki/Anarcho-syndicalists "Anarcho-syndicalists") and [socialists](/wiki/Socialists "Socialists").[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72)

In 1936, Trotsky argued in his work, *[The Revolution Betrayed](/wiki/The_Revolution_Betrayed "The Revolution Betrayed")*, for the restoration of the right of criticism in areas such as [economic matters](/wiki/Economic_policy "Economic policy"), the revitalization of [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_unions "Trade unions") and free elections of the [Soviet parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_Soviet_Union "List of political parties in the Soviet Union").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotsky1991218-10) Trotsky also argued that the excessive [authoritarianism](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") under Stalin had undermined the implementation of the [First five-year plan](/wiki/First_five-year_plan_(Soviet_Union) "First five-year plan (Soviet Union)"). He noted that several engineers and economists who had created the plan were themselves later put on trial as "[conscious wreckers](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge") who had acted on the instructions of a foreign power".[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotsky1991[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidhiCYS9Z3lDoCqtrotskytheengineersandeconomistswhocreatedthisplanwereafewyearslatersternlypunished_28]-73)

Polish historian and biographer, [Isaac Deutscher](/wiki/Isaac_Deutscher "Isaac Deutscher"), viewed his inner-party reforms in 1923–24 as arguably the first act in the restoration of free Soviet institutions which the party had sought to establish in 1917 and the return of [worker's democracy](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)") which would correspond with a gradual dismantlement of the [single-party system](/wiki/One-party_system "One-party system").[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015674–676-74)
At the same time, Deutscher noted that Trotsky's attitude towards democracy could be characterised as inconsistent and hesitant by opponents, but this stemmed from a range of reasons, such as the [ill timing after the failed revolutions in the West](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1917%E2%80%931923 "Revolutions of 1917–1923") and controversies around party schisms.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015674–676-74)

In the *[Transitional Program](/wiki/The_Death_Agony_of_Capitalism_and_the_Tasks_of_the_Fourth_International "The Death Agony of Capitalism and the Tasks of the Fourth International")*, which was drafted in 1938 during the founding congress of the [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International"), Trotsky reiterated the need for the legalization of the [Soviet parties](/wiki/Second_All-Russian_Congress_of_Soviets_of_Workers%27_and_Soldiers%27_Deputies "Second All-Russian Congress of Soviets of Workers' and Soldiers' Deputies") and [worker's control of production](/wiki/Workers_control "Workers control").[[7]](#cite_note-books.google.com-7)

### Uneven and combined development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Uneven and combined development")]

Main article: [Uneven and combined development](/wiki/Uneven_and_combined_development "Uneven and combined development")

The concept of uneven and combined development was derived from the political theories of Trotsky.[[74]](#cite_note-75) This concept was developed in combination with the related theory of permanent revolution to explain the historical context of Russia. He would later elaborate on this theory to explain the specific laws of uneven development in 1930 and the conditions for a possible revolutionary scenario.[[75]](#cite_note-76) According to biographer Ian Thatcher, this theory would be later generalised to "the entire history of mankind".[[76]](#cite_note-77)

Political scientists Emanuele Saccarelli and Latha Varadarajan valued his theory as a "signal contribution" to the discipline of [international relations](/wiki/International_relations "International relations"). They argued his theory presented "a specific understanding of capitalist development as "uneven", insofar as it systematically featured geographically divergent "advanced" and "backward" regions" across the [world economy](/wiki/World_economy "World economy").[[77]](#cite_note-78)

### Socialist culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Socialist culture")]

Main articles: [Literature and Revolution](/wiki/Literature_and_Revolution "Literature and Revolution"), [Problems of Everyday Life](/wiki/Problems_of_Everyday_Life "Problems of Everyday Life"), and [New York Intellectuals](/wiki/New_York_Intellectuals "New York Intellectuals")

> Faith merely promises to move mountains; but [technology](/wiki/Technology "Technology"), which takes nothing ‘on faith’, is actually able to cut down mountains and move them. Up to now this was done for industrial purposes (mines) or for railways (tunnels); in the future this will be done on an immeasurably larger scale, according to a general industrial and [artistic](/wiki/Artistic "Artistic") plan. Man will occupy himself with re-registering mountains and rivers, and will earnestly and repeatedly make improvements in [nature](/wiki/Nature "Nature").

—Trotsky, *Literature and Revolution*, 1924[[78]](#cite_note-79)

In *[Literature and Revolution](/wiki/Literature_and_Revolution "Literature and Revolution")*, Trotsky examined aesthetic issues concerning class and the Russian Revolution. Soviet scholar Robert Bird considered his work the "first systematic treatment of art by a Communist leader" and a catalyst for later Marxist cultural and critical theories.[[79]](#cite_note-80) He had also defended intellectual autonomy in relation to the Russian [literary movements](/wiki/Literary_movements "Literary movements") and scientific theories such as [Freudian psychoanalytic theory](/wiki/Freud%27s_psychoanalytic_theories "Freud's psychoanalytic theories") along with [Einstein](/wiki/Einstein "Einstein")’s [theory of relativity](/wiki/Theory_of_relativity "Theory of relativity") during the succession period. However, these theories were increasingly marginalised during the Stalin era.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015729–730-81)

In *[Problems of Everyday Life](/wiki/Problems_of_Everyday_Life "Problems of Everyday Life")*, a contemporaneous book which further articulated his views on culture and science, Trotsky argued that cultural development would accentuate industrial and technical progress. He viewed both elements to be interrelated components as part of dialectical interaction in which he viewed the low level of Russian [technique](/wiki/Technology "Technology") and [expertise](/wiki/Expertise "Expertise") to be a function of cultural backwardness. According to Trotsky, Western industrial methods and products such as the radio should not be rejected due to their status as products of a capitalist system, but rather absorbed into the Soviet socialist framework to facilitate new forms of techniques and cultural production.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978282–300-82) In this interpretation, the [transference of techniques](/wiki/Technology_transfer "Technology transfer") brought new cultural changes in terms of rationalism, [efficiency](/wiki/Efficiency "Efficiency"), exactitude, and [quality](/wiki/Service_quality "Service quality").[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978282–300-82)

Trotsky would later co-author the 1938 *[Manifesto for an Independent Revolutionary Art](/wiki/Manifesto_for_an_Independent_Revolutionary_Art "Manifesto for an Independent Revolutionary Art")* with the endorsement of prominent artists [Andre Breton](/wiki/Andre_Breton "Andre Breton") and [Diego Rivera](/wiki/Diego_Rivera "Diego Rivera").[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher20151474–1475-83) Trotsky's writings on literature such as his 1923 survey which advocated tolerance, limited censorship and respect for literary tradition had strong appeal to the [New York Intellectuals](/wiki/New_York_Intellectuals "New York Intellectuals").[[83]](#cite_note-84)

Trotsky critiqued contemporary literary movements such as [Futurism](/wiki/Futurism "Futurism") and emphasised the need for cultural autonomy to develop a socialist culture. According to literary critic [Terry Eagleton](/wiki/Terry_Eagleton "Terry Eagleton"), Trotsky recognised "like Lenin on the need for a socialist culture to absorb the finest products of bourgeois art".[[15]](#cite_note-Marxism_and_Literary_Criticism-15) Trotsky himself viewed the proletarian culture as "temporary and transitional" which would provide the foundations for a culture above classes. He also argued that the preconditions for artistic creativity were economic well-being and emancipation from material constraints.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978289–301-85)

Political scientist, Baruch Knei-Paz, characterised [his view](/wiki/The_Social_and_Political_Thought_of_Leon_Trotsky "The Social and Political Thought of Leon Trotsky") on the role of the party as transmitters of culture to the masses and raising the standards of education, as well as entry into the cultural sphere, but that the process of artistic creation in terms of language and presentation should be the domain of the practitioner. Knei-Paz also noted key distinctions between Trotsky's approach to cultural matters and [Stalin's policy in the 1930s](/wiki/Socialist_realism#Stalin_era "Socialist realism").[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978289–301-85)

### Economics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Economics")]

Main articles: [Economic planning](/wiki/Economic_planning "Economic planning"), [Socialist economics](/wiki/Socialist_economics "Socialist economics"), [Planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy"), [Mechanised agriculture](/wiki/Mechanised_agriculture "Mechanised agriculture"), [Left Opposition](/wiki/Left_Opposition "Left Opposition"), [Scissors Crisis](/wiki/Scissors_Crisis "Scissors Crisis"), and [Soviet grain procurement crisis of 1928](/wiki/Soviet_grain_procurement_crisis_of_1928 "Soviet grain procurement crisis of 1928")

Trotsky was an early proponent of economic planning since 1923 and favored an accelerated pace of industrialization.[[85]](#cite_note-Twiss-86) In 1921, he had also been a prominent supporter of [Gosplan](/wiki/Gosplan "Gosplan") as a newly established body and called for the strengthening of its formal responsibilities to support a balanced level of economic reconstruction after the Civil War.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015587-87) Lenin and [Politburo](/wiki/Politburo "Politburo") members had also proposed he served as deputy chairman with a focus on economic matters related to the either [STO](/wiki/Council_of_Labor_and_Defence#New_name,_new_role "Council of Labor and Defence"), Gosplan or the Council of National Economy.[[87]](#cite_note-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89)

Trotsky had urged economic [decentralisation](/wiki/Decentralisation "Decentralisation") between the state, [oblast](/wiki/Oblast "Oblast") regions, and factories to counter structural inefficiency and the problem of bureaucracy.[[6]](#cite_note-BRILL-6) He had proposed the principles underlying the [N.E.P.](/wiki/New_Economic_Policy "New Economic Policy") in 1920 to the [Politburo](/wiki/Politburo "Politburo") to mitigate urgent economic matters arising from war communism. He would later reproach Lenin privately about the delayed government response in 1921–1922.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015507–508,_585-90)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERubenstein2011[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleontrotskyrevol0000rubepage160mode2upqforcedcollectivization_161]-17) However, his position also differed from the majority of Soviet leaders at the time who fully supported the [New Economic policy](/wiki/New_Economic_policy "New Economic policy").[[85]](#cite_note-Twiss-86)[[90]](#cite_note-91)

Comparatively, Trotsky believed that planning and N.E.P. should develop within a mixed framework until the socialist sector gradually superseded the private industry.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015646-92) He found allies among a circle of economic theorists and administrators, which included [Evgenii Preobazhensky](/wiki/Yevgeni_Preobrazhensky "Yevgeni Preobrazhensky") along [Georgy Pyatakov](/wiki/Georgy_Pyatakov "Georgy Pyatakov"), deputy chairman of the [Council of the National Economy](/wiki/Supreme_Soviet_of_the_National_Economy "Supreme Soviet of the National Economy")[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015592-93) and had more broadly the support of many party [intellectuals](/wiki/Intellectuals "Intellectuals").[[93]](#cite_note-94)

Trotsky had specified the need for the "overall guidance in planning i.e. the systematic co-ordination of the fundamental sectors of the state economy in the process of adapting to the present market" and urged for a national plan[[94]](#cite_note-95) alongside currency stabilization.[[95]](#cite_note-96) He also rejected the Stalinist conception of industrialisation, which favoured [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry"). Instead, he proposed the use of [foreign trade](/wiki/Foreign_trade "Foreign trade") as an accelerator and to direct investments by means of a system of comparative [coefficients](/wiki/Coefficients "Coefficients").[[96]](#cite_note-97)

> The problem of agriculture is a much more complicated one, and there is nothing surprising in this to the Marxian mind. The change from the system of small individual peasant holdings to socialist methods of land cultivation is only conceivable after a number of consecutive stages of progress in technical science in economics and culture.

-Trotsky on the need for a gradual and scientific [socialization](/wiki/Social_ownership "Social ownership") of agriculture in *“[Towards Socialism or Capitalism?](/wiki/Towards_Socialism_or_Capitalism%3F "Towards Socialism or Capitalism?")”*, 1926.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

Trotsky and the Left Opposition developed a number of economic proposals in response to the [scissor crisis](/wiki/Scissor_crisis "Scissor crisis"), which had undermined relations between the workers and peasants in 1923–1924. This included a [progressive tax](/wiki/Progressive_tax "Progressive tax") on the wealthier sections of populations, such as the [kulaks](/wiki/Kulak "Kulak") and [NEPmen](/wiki/NEPmen "NEPmen"), alongside an equilibrium of the import-export balance to access accumulated reserves to purchase machinery from abroad to increase the pace of industrialization.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMandel199562-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100) The policy was later adopted by members of the United Opposition, which also advocated a programme of rapid industrialization during the debates of 1924 and 1927. The United Opposition proposed a progressive tax on wealthier peasants, the encouragement of [agricultural cooperatives](/wiki/Agricultural_cooperatives "Agricultural cooperatives"), and the formation of collective farms voluntarily.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

Trotsky, as president of the [electrification](/wiki/Electrification "Electrification") commission, along with members of the Opposition bloc, had also put forward an electrification plan which involved the construction of the [hydroelectric](/wiki/Hydroelectric "Hydroelectric") [Dnieprostroi dam](/wiki/Dnieper_Hydroelectric_Station "Dnieper Hydroelectric Station").[[101]](#cite_note-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103) According to historian [Sheila Fitzpatrick](/wiki/Sheila_Fitzpatrick "Sheila Fitzpatrick"), the scholarly consensus was that Stalin appropriated the position of the Left Opposition on such matters as [industrialisation](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") and [collectivisation](/wiki/Collectivisation "Collectivisation").[[103]](#cite_note-104) In exile, Trotsky maintained that the disproportions and imbalances which became characteristic of Stalinist planning in the 1930s such as the underdeveloped [consumer base](/wiki/Consumption_(economics) "Consumption (economics)") along with the priority focus on [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry") were due to a number of avoidable problems. He argued that the industrial drive had been enacted under more severe circumstances, several years later, and in a less rational manner than originally conceived by the Left Opposition.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher20151141-105)

[Ernest Mandel](/wiki/Ernest_Mandel "Ernest Mandel") argued that the economic programme of Trotsky differed from the forced [policy of collectivisation](/wiki/Collectivization_in_the_Soviet_Union "Collectivization in the Soviet Union") implemented by Stalin after 1928 due to the levels of brutality associated with its enforcement.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMandel199559-106) Notably, Trotsky sought to raise taxation on wealthier farmers and encourage farm labourers, along with poor peasants to form collective farms voluntarily in conjunction with the allocation of state resources to agricultural [machinery](/wiki/Machinery "Machinery"), [fertilizers](/wiki/Fertilizers "Fertilizers"), [credit](/wiki/Credit "Credit") and [agronomic](/wiki/Agronomic "Agronomic") assistance.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher20151130-107)

In 1932–33, Trotsky insisted upon the need for mass participation in the operationalisation of the planned economy:

> "Insoluble without the daily experience of millions, without their critical review of their own collective experience, without their expression of their needs and demands and could not be carried out within the confines of the official sanctums.... even if the [Politburo](/wiki/Politburo "Politburo") consisted of seven universal geniuses, of seven Marxes, or seven Lenins, it will still be unable, all on its own, with all its creative imagination, to assert command over the [economy](/wiki/Economy "Economy") of 170 million people".[[107]](#cite_note-108)

British [cybernetician](/wiki/Cybernetician "Cybernetician") [Stafford Beer](/wiki/Stafford_Beer "Stafford Beer"), who worked on a [decentralized](/wiki/Decentralization "Decentralization") form of economic planning, [Project Cybersyn](/wiki/Project_Cybersyn "Project Cybersyn") from 1970 to 1973, was reported to have read and been influenced by Trotsky's critique of the [Soviet bureaucracy](/wiki/Nomenklatura "Nomenklatura").[[108]](#cite_note-109)

The economic platform of a [planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy") combined with an authentic [worker's democracy](/wiki/Socialist_democracy "Socialist democracy") as originally advocated by Trotsky has constituted the programme of the Fourth International and the modern Trotskyist movement.[[109]](#cite_note-110)

### Transitional program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Transitional program")]

Main article: [The Transitional Program](/wiki/The_Transitional_Program "The Transitional Program")

Trotsky drafted the transitional programme as a programmatic document for the founding congress of the Fourth International in 1938.[[110]](#cite_note-Pathfinder_Press-111)

He explicitly emphasised the core need for the programme:

> "It is explicitly necessary to help the masses in the process of the daily struggle to find the bridge between present demands and the socialist program of the revolution. This bridge should include a system of transitional demands, stemming from today's conditions and from today's consciousness of wide layers of the working class and unalterably leading to one final conclusion: the conquest of power by the proletariat".[[110]](#cite_note-Pathfinder_Press-111)

The transitional programme features three types of proposals for action. This includes democratic demands such as [the right of unions](/wiki/Labor_rights "Labor rights") and [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination"); immediate demands which are concerned with everyday struggles such as [wage increases](/wiki/Equal_pay "Equal pay") and transitional demands which are directed at the capitalist system such as [worker's control of production](/wiki/Workers%27_council "Workers' council") .[[110]](#cite_note-Pathfinder_Press-111)

### Scientific socialism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Scientific socialism")]

> We must give a scientific explanation of society, and clearly explain it to the masses. That is the difference between Marxism and reformism.

—Trotsky on the transitional program of the [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International").[[111]](#cite_note-112)

In his collection of texts, *[In Defence of Marxism](/wiki/In_Defence_of_Marxism_(book) "In Defence of Marxism (book)")*, [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") defended the [dialectical method](/wiki/Dialectical_materialism "Dialectical materialism") of scientific socialism during the factional schisms within the American Trotskyist movement during 1939–40. Trotsky viewed dialectics as an essential method of analysis to discern the class nature of the Soviet Union. Specifically, he described scientific socialism as the "conscious expression of the unconscious historical process".[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotsky201931,_68–70,_138-113) According to Daniels, Trotsky conceived historical revolutions as a long, interrelated process of political and social struggle which undergo various stages with national and international dimensions.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels200883–92-114)

Prior to his expulsion from the Soviet Union, Trotsky had encouraged [empirical](/wiki/Empirical "Empirical") studies with the use of Marxist methods for social and historical development. He also insisted on the need for a [freedom of science](/wiki/Intellectual_freedom "Intellectual freedom"), including theoretical research, in 1925, for socially useful purposes.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978476–495-115) Trotsky defended [Einstein](/wiki/Einstein "Einstein")’s [theory of relativity](/wiki/Theory_of_relativity "Theory of relativity") in Soviet intellectual circles, but this became an anathema during the Stalin era and was only rehabilitated following the latter's death.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher201530-116)

In line with the scientific outlook of Marxist philosophy, Trotsky heavily emphasized science to alleviate the level of backwardness among the Soviet masses. Concurrently, he viewed socialism as a progressive struggle for [science](/wiki/Science "Science"), culture, and morality in which science should be given the maximum scope for development.[[116]](#cite_note-117) In 1918, he had supported [Taylorism](/wiki/Taylorism "Taylorism") along with Lenin as a means of scientifically managing industries with the support of foreign engineers.[[117]](#cite_note-118) Multiple historians have stressed the [technocratic](/wiki/Technocratic "Technocratic") nature of his governance proposals compared to Stalin and Bukharin with a higher reliance on "bourgeois" experts and specialists.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwain2014[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid_a3pAgAAQBAJdqtrotsky27ssovietunionwouldhavebeenfarmoretechnocraticpgPA118_118]-119)[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels2008189-120)[[120]](#cite_note-121)

Trotskyists have emphasised the dialectical relationship between [objective](/wiki/Objectivity_(science) "Objectivity (science)"), [material conditions](/wiki/Material_conditions "Material conditions"), and [subjective factors](/wiki/Subjectivity_and_objectivity "Subjectivity and objectivity") such as party, leadership, in understanding the nature of change in historical events.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

### United front and theory of fascism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: United front and theory of fascism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1f/Fascism.png)](/wiki/File:Fascism.png)

Leon Trotsky's original pamphlet ***"[Fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism"): What it is and how to fight it"*** argued for the tactical method of a united front to counter the rise of [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany")

Main articles: [United front](/wiki/United_front "United front") and [The Struggle Against Fascism in Germany](/wiki/The_Struggle_Against_Fascism_in_Germany "The Struggle Against Fascism in Germany")

> At a time when hundreds of thousands and millions of workers, especially in Germany, are departing from Communism, in part to fascism and in the main into the camp of indifferentism, thousands and tens of thousands of Social Democratic workers, under the impact of the self-same defeat, are evolving into the left, to the side of Communism. There cannot, however, even be talk of their accepting the hopelessly discredited Stalinist leadership.

—Trotsky's writings on the challenge of Stalinism and fascism in 1933.[[122]](#cite_note-123)

Trotsky was a central figure in the [Comintern](/wiki/Comintern "Comintern") during its first four congresses. During this time, he helped to generalize the strategy and tactics of the Bolsheviks to newly formed Communist parties across Europe and further afield. From 1921 onwards, the [united front](/wiki/United_front "United front"), a method of uniting revolutionaries and reformists in the common struggle while winning some of the workers to revolution, was the central tactic put forward by the Comintern after the defeat of the German revolution.

After he was exiled and politically marginalized by Stalinism, Trotsky continued to argue for a united front against fascism in Germany and Spain. According to Joseph Choonara of the British [Socialist Workers Party](/wiki/Socialist_Workers_Party_(UK) "Socialist Workers Party (UK)") in *[International Socialism](/wiki/International_Socialism_(magazine) "International Socialism (magazine)")*, his articles on the united front represent an essential part of his political legacy.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

Trotsky also formulated a theory of fascism based on a dialectical interpretation of events to analyze the manifestation of [Italian fascism](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism") and the early emergence of Nazi Germany from 1930 to 1933.[[124]](#cite_note-125)

Marxist theorist and economist [Hillel Ticktin](/wiki/Hillel_Ticktin "Hillel Ticktin") argued that his political strategy and approach to fascism such as the emphasis on an organisational bloc between the [German Communist Party](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Germany "Communist Party of Germany") and [Social-Democratic party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") during the interwar period would very likely have prevented [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") [from ascending to political power](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler%27s_rise_to_power#Weimar_parties_fail_to_halt_Nazis "Adolf Hitler's rise to power").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETicktin1992227-14)

### Political ethics and morality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Political ethics and morality")]

> A means can be justified only by its end. But the end in turn needs to be justified. From the Marxist point of view, which expresses the historical interests of the proletariat, the end is justified if it leads to increasing the power of man over nature and to the abolition of the power of man over man.

—Trotsky's writings on "The Dialectical Interpedence of Ends and Means".[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978559-126)

In 1938, Trotsky had written *[Their Morals and Ours](/wiki/Their_Morals_and_Ours:_The_class_foundations_of_moral_practice "Their Morals and Ours: The class foundations of moral practice")* which consisted of [ethical polemics](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics") in response to criticisms around his actions concerning the [Kronstadt rebellion](/wiki/Kronstadt_rebellion "Kronstadt rebellion") and wider questions posed around the perceived, "[amoral](/wiki/Amorality "Amorality")" methods of the Bolsheviks. Critics believed these methods seemed to emulate the [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") [maxim](/wiki/Maxim_(philosophy) "Maxim (philosophy)") that the "[ends justifies the means](/wiki/Consequentialism "Consequentialism")". Trotsky argued that Marxism situated the foundation of morality as a product of society to [serve social interests](/wiki/Marxian_class_theory "Marxian class theory") rather than "eternal moral truths" proclaimed by institutional religions.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978556–560-127) On the other hand, he regarded it as farcical to assert that an end could justify any criminal means and viewed this to be a distorted representation of the Jesuit maxim. Instead, Trotsky believed that the means and ends frequently “exchanged places” as when [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") is sought by the [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") as an instrument to actualize socialism. He also viewed revolution to be deducible from the [laws of the development](/wiki/Dialectical_materialism "Dialectical materialism") and primarily the [class struggle](/wiki/Class_struggle "Class struggle"), but this did not mean all means are permissible.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978556–560-127) Fundamentally, Trotsky argued that ends "rejects" means that are incompatible with itself.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher20151482-128) In other words, [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") cannot be furthered through [fraud](/wiki/Fraud "Fraud"), [deceit](/wiki/The_Stalin_School_of_Falsification "The Stalin School of Falsification") or [the worship of leaders](/wiki/Stalin%27s_cult_of_personality "Stalin's cult of personality") but through honesty and integrity as essential elements of [revolutionary](/wiki/Revolutionary "Revolutionary") morality in dealing with the working masses.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher20151482-128)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Leo_Trotzki_1900_in_Sibirien.jpg/250px-Leo_Trotzki_1900_in_Sibirien.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leo_Trotzki_1900_in_Sibirien.jpg)

Trotsky in exile in Siberia, 1900

According to Trotsky, the term "Trotskyism" was coined by [Pavel Milyukov](/wiki/Pavel_Milyukov "Pavel Milyukov") (sometimes transliterated as Paul Miliukoff), the ideological leader of the [Constitutional Democratic Party (Kadets)](/wiki/Constitutional_Democratic_Party "Constitutional Democratic Party") in Russia. Milyukov waged a bitter war against Trotskyism "as early as 1905".[[128]](#cite_note-129)

Trotsky was elected chairman of the [St. Petersburg Soviet](/wiki/St._Petersburg_Soviet "St. Petersburg Soviet") during the [Russian Revolution of 1905](/wiki/Russian_Revolution_of_1905 "Russian Revolution of 1905"). He pursued a policy of [proletarian revolution](/wiki/Proletarian_revolution "Proletarian revolution") at a time when other socialist trends advocated a transition to a "bourgeois" (capitalist) regime to replace the essentially feudal Romanov state. This year, Trotsky developed the theory of [permanent revolution](/wiki/Permanent_revolution "Permanent revolution"), as it later became known (see below). In 1905, Trotsky quotes from a postscript to a book by Milyukov, *The Elections to the Second State Duma*, published no later than May 1907:

> Those who reproach the Kadets with failure to protest at that time, by organising meetings, against the "revolutionary illusions" of Trotskyism and the relapse into [Blanquism](/wiki/Blanquism "Blanquism"), simply do not understand [...] the mood of the democratic public at meetings during that period.

— Pavel Milyukov, *The Elections to the Second State Duma*[[129]](#cite_note-130)

Milyukov suggests that the mood of the "democratic public" was in support of Trotsky's policy of the overthrow of the Romanov regime alongside a workers' revolution to overthrow the capitalist owners of industry, support for strike action and the establishment of democratically elected [workers' councils](/wiki/Workers%27_council "Workers' council") or "soviets". This differed from variations of [Council Communism](/wiki/Council_communism "Council communism") in [Germany](/wiki/German_Communist_Party "German Communist Party") due to the Russian Peasantry and the role they have in overall [Leninism](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism") including Trotskyism compared to the role they have in Council Communism

### Trotskyism and the 1917 Russian Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Trotskyism and the 1917 Russian Revolution")]

During his leadership of the Russian revolution of 1905, Trotsky argued that once it became clear that the Tsar's army would not come out in support of the workers, it was necessary to retreat before the armed might of the state in as good an order as possible.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotsky1971217_ff-131) In 1917, Trotsky was again elected chairman of the Petrograd soviet, but this time soon came to lead the [Military Revolutionary Committee](/wiki/Military_Revolutionary_Committee "Military Revolutionary Committee"), which had the allegiance of the Petrograd garrison and carried through the October 1917 insurrection. Stalin wrote:

> All practical work in connection with the organisation of the uprising was done under the immediate direction of Comrade Trotsky, the President of the Petrograd Soviet. It can be stated with certainty that the Party is indebted primarily and principally to Comrade Trotsky for the rapid going over of the garrison to the side of the Soviet and the efficient manner in which the work of the Military Revolutionary Committee was organized. The principal assistants of Comrade Trotsky were Comrades Antonov and Podvoisky.

— Joseph Stalin, *[Pravda](/wiki/Pravda "Pravda")*, November 6, 1918[[131]](#cite_note-132)

As a result of his role in the Russian Revolution of 1917, the theory of permanent revolution was embraced by the young Soviet state until 1924.

The Russian revolution of 1917 was marked by two revolutions: the relatively spontaneous February 1917 revolution and the 25 October 1917 seizure of power by the Bolsheviks, who had gained the leadership of the Petrograd soviet.

Before the February 1917 Russian revolution, Lenin had formulated a slogan calling for the "democratic dictatorship of the proletariat and the peasantry", but after the February revolution, through his April Theses, Lenin instead called for "all power to the Soviets". Nevertheless, Lenin continued to emphasise (as did Trotsky) the classical Marxist position that the peasantry formed a basis for the development of capitalism, not socialism.[[132]](#cite_note-133)

Also, before February 1917, Trotsky had not accepted the importance of a Bolshevik-style organisation. Once the February 1917 Russian revolution had broken out, Trotsky admitted the importance of a Bolshevik organisation and joined the Bolsheviks in July 1917. Although many, like Stalin, saw Trotsky's role in the October 1917 Russian revolution as central, Trotsky wrote that without Lenin and the Bolshevik Party, the October revolution of 1917 would not have taken place.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/19191107-lenin_second_anniversary_october_revolution_moscow.jpg/250px-19191107-lenin_second_anniversary_october_revolution_moscow.jpg)](/wiki/File:19191107-lenin_second_anniversary_october_revolution_moscow.jpg)

[Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin"), [Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") and [Kamenev](/wiki/Lev_Kamenev "Lev Kamenev") celebrating the second anniversary of the October Revolution

Other Bolshevik figures such as [Anatoly Lunacharsky](/wiki/Anatoly_Lunacharsky "Anatoly Lunacharsky"), [Moisei Uritsky](/wiki/Moisei_Uritsky "Moisei Uritsky") and [Dmitry Manuilsky](/wiki/Dmitry_Manuilsky "Dmitry Manuilsky") agreed that Lenin's influence on the Bolshevik party was decisive but the October insurrection was carried out according to Trotsky's, not to Lenin's plan.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher20151283-134)

As a result, since 1917, Trotskyism as a political theory has been fully committed to a Leninist style of [democratic centralist](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism") party organisation, which Trotskyists argue must not be confused with the party organisation as it later developed under Stalin. Trotsky had previously suggested that Lenin's method of organisation would lead to a dictatorship. However, it is essential to emphasise that after 1917, orthodox Trotskyists argue that the loss of democracy in the USSR was caused by the failure of the revolution to spread internationally and the consequent wars, isolation, and imperialist intervention, not the Bolshevik style of organisation.

> After the majority of the petrograd Soviet passed into the hands of the Bolsheviks, [Trotsky] was elected its chairman and in that position organized and led the insurrection of October 25.

Lenin on the organization of the October Revolution, Vol.XIV of the *Collected Works*.[[134]](#cite_note-135)

Lenin's outlook had always been that the Russian revolution would need to stimulate a Socialist revolution in Western Europe so that this European socialist society would come to the aid of the Russian revolution and enable Russia to advance towards socialism. Lenin stated:

> We have stressed in a good many written works, in all our public utterances, and in all our statements in the press that [...] the socialist revolution can triumph only on two conditions. First, if it is given timely support by a socialist revolution in one or several advanced countries.

— Vladimir Lenin, Speech at Tenth Congress of the RCP(B)[[135]](#cite_note-136)

This outlook matched Trotsky's theory of permanent revolution precisely. Trotsky's permanent revolution had foreseen that the working class would not stop at the bourgeois democratic stage of the revolution but proceed towards a workers' state, as happened in 1917. The Polish Trotskyist [Isaac Deutscher](/wiki/Isaac_Deutscher "Isaac Deutscher") maintains that in 1917, Lenin changed his attitude toward Trotsky's theory of Permanent Revolution, and after the October revolution, it was adopted by the Bolsheviks.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher1966285-137)

Lenin was met with initial disbelief in April 1917. Trotsky argues that:

> [...] up to the outbreak of the February revolution and for a time after Trotskyism did not mean the idea that it was impossible to build a socialist society within the national boundaries of Russia (which "possibility" was never expressed by anybody up to 1924 and hardly came into anybody's head). Trotskyism meant the idea that the Russian proletariat might win the power in advance of the Western proletariat, and that in that case it could not confine itself within the limits of a democratic dictatorship but would be compelled to undertake the initial socialist measures. It is not surprising, then, that the April theses of Lenin were condemned as Trotskyist.

— Leon Trotsky, *History of the Russian Revolution*[[137]](#cite_note-138)

### "Legend of Trotskyism"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: \"Legend of Trotskyism\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Leon_Trotsky.JPG/250px-Leon_Trotsky.JPG)](/wiki/File:Leon_Trotsky.JPG)

"Bolshevik freedom" with nude of Trotsky in a Polish propaganda poster, Polish–Soviet War (1920)

In *[The Stalin School of Falsification](/wiki/The_Stalin_School_of_Falsification "The Stalin School of Falsification")*, Trotsky argues that what he calls the "legend of Trotskyism" was formulated by [Grigory Zinoviev](/wiki/Grigory_Zinoviev "Grigory Zinoviev") and [Lev Kamenev](/wiki/Lev_Kamenev "Lev Kamenev") in collaboration with Stalin in 1924 in response to the criticisms Trotsky raised of Politburo policy.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher1966293-139) [Orlando Figes](/wiki/Orlando_Figes "Orlando Figes") argues: "The urge to silence Trotsky, and all criticism of the Politburo, was in itself a crucial factor in Stalin's rise to power".[[139]](#cite_note-140)

During 1922–1924, Lenin suffered a series of strokes and became increasingly incapacitated. In [a document](/wiki/Lenin%27s_Testament "Lenin's Testament") dictated before his death in 1924 while describing Trotsky as "distinguished not only by his exceptional abilities—personally he is, to be sure, the most able man in the present Central Committee" and also maintaining that "his non-Bolshevik past should not be held against him", Lenin criticized him for "showing excessive preoccupation with the purely administrative side of the work" and also requested that Stalin be removed from his position of General Secretary, but his notes remained suppressed until 1956.[[140]](#cite_note-141) Zinoviev and Kamenev broke with Stalin in 1925 and joined Trotsky in 1926 in what was known as the [United Opposition](/wiki/United_Opposition_(Soviet_Union) "United Opposition (Soviet Union)").[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotsky1971[httpwwwmarxistsorgarchivetrotsky1937ssfsf07htm_89]-142)

In 1926, Stalin allied with [Nikolai Bukharin](/wiki/Nikolai_Bukharin "Nikolai Bukharin"), who led the campaign against "Trotskyism". In *The Stalin School of Falsification*, Trotsky quotes Bukharin's 1918 pamphlet, *From the Collapse of Czarism to the Fall of the Bourgeoisie*, which was re-printed in 1923 by the party publishing house, Proletari. Bukharin explains and embraces Trotsky's theory of permanent revolution in this pamphlet: "The Russian proletariat is confronted more sharply than ever before with the problem of the international revolution ... The grand total of relationships which have arisen in Europe leads to this inevitable conclusion. Thus, the permanent revolution in Russia is passing into the European proletarian revolution". Yet it is common knowledge, Trotsky argues, that three years later in 1926 "Bukharin was the chief and indeed the sole theoretician of the entire campaign against 'Trotskyism', summed up in the struggle against the theory of the permanent revolution."[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotsky1971[httpwwwmarxistsorgarchivetrotsky1937ssfsf06htm_78]-143)

Trotsky wrote that the [Left Opposition](/wiki/Left_Opposition "Left Opposition") grew in influence throughout the 1920s, attempting to reform the Communist Party, but in 1927 Stalin declared "civil war" against them:

> During the first ten years of its struggle, the Left Opposition did not abandon the program of ideological conquest of the party for that of conquest of power against the party. Its slogan was: reform, not revolution. The bureaucracy, however, even in those times, was ready for any revolution in order to defend itself against a democratic reform.
>
> In 1927, when the struggle reached an especially bitter stage, Stalin declared at a session of the Central Committee, addressing himself to the Opposition: "Those cadres can be removed only by civil war!" What was a threat in Stalin's words became, thanks to a series of defeats of the European proletariat, a historic fact. The road of reform was turned into a road of revolution.

— Leon Trotsky, [[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotsky1991279-144)

Internationally, Trotsky's opposition and criticism of the ruling troika received support from several, Central Committee members of foreign communist parties. This included [Christian Rakovsky](/wiki/Christian_Rakovsky "Christian Rakovsky"), [Chairman](/wiki/Council_of_People%27s_Commissars_(Ukraine) "Council of People's Commissars (Ukraine)") of the [Ukraine Sovnarkom](/wiki/Ukrainian_SSR "Ukrainian SSR"), [Boris Souvarine](/wiki/Boris_Souvarine "Boris Souvarine") of the [French Communist Party](/wiki/French_Communist_Party "French Communist Party") and the Central Committee of the [Polish Communist Party](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Poland "Communist Party of Poland") which was led by prominent theoreticians such as [Maksymilian Horwitz](/wiki/Maksymilian_Horwitz "Maksymilian Horwitz"), [Maria Koszutska](/wiki/Maria_Koszutska "Maria Koszutska") and [Adolf Warski](/wiki/Adolf_Warski "Adolf Warski").[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogovin2021139,_249,_268–269-145)

The defeat of the European working class led to further isolation in Russia and further suppression of the Opposition. Trotsky argued that the "so-called struggle against 'Trotskyism' grew out of the bureaucratic reaction against the October Revolution [of 1917]".[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotsky1971xxxiiiForeword_to_the_Russian_edition-146) He responded to the one-sided civil war with his *Letter to the Bureau of Party History* (1927), contrasting what he claimed to be the falsification of history with the official history of just a few years before. He further accused Stalin of derailing the [Chinese Communist Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution") and causing the massacre of the Chinese workers:

> In the year 1918, Stalin, at the very outset of his campaign against me, found it necessary, as we have already learned, to write the following words:
>
> > "All the work of practical organization of the insurrection was carried out under the direct leadership of the Chairman of the Petrograd Soviet, comrade Trotsky..." (Stalin, *Pravda*, 6 November 1918)
>
> With full responsibility for my words, I am now compelled to say that the cruel massacre of the Chinese proletariat and the Chinese Revolution at its three most important turning points, the strengthening of the position of the trade union agents of British imperialism after the [General Strike of 1926](/wiki/1926_United_Kingdom_general_strike "1926 United Kingdom general strike"), and, finally, the general weakening of the position of the Communist International and the Soviet Union, the party owes principally and above all to Stalin.

— Leon Trotsky, [[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotsky197187-147)

Trotsky was sent into internal exile, and his supporters were jailed. For instance, Victor Serge first "spent six weeks in a cell" after a visit at midnight, then 85 days in an inner [GPU](/wiki/State_Political_Directorate "State Political Directorate") cell, most of it in solitary confinement.
