He details the jailings of the Left Opposition.[[147]](#cite_note-Serge_1973-148) However, the Left Opposition worked secretly within the USSR.[[147]](#cite_note-Serge_1973-148) Trotsky was eventually exiled to Turkey and moved to France, Norway and finally Mexico.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher1966381-149)

After 1928, the various Communist Parties worldwide expelled Trotskyists from their ranks. Most Trotskyists defend the economic achievements of the planned economy in the USSR during the 1920s and 1930s, despite the "misleadership" of the Soviet bureaucracy and what they claim to be the loss of democracy.[[149]](#cite_note-150) Trotskyists claim that in 1928 inner party democracy and soviet democracy, which was at the foundation of Bolshevism,[[150]](#cite_note-151) had been destroyed within the various Communist Parties. Anyone who disagreed with the party line was labelled a Trotskyist and even a [fascist](/wiki/Fascist_(insult) "Fascist (insult)").

### Founding of the Fourth International

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Founding of the Fourth International")]

Main articles: [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International") and [Entryism](/wiki/Entryism "Entryism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Lenin%2C_Trotsky_and_Voroshilov_with_Delegates_of_the_10th_Congress_of_the_Russian_Communist_Party_%28Bolsheviks%29.jpg/250px-Lenin%2C_Trotsky_and_Voroshilov_with_Delegates_of_the_10th_Congress_of_the_Russian_Communist_Party_%28Bolsheviks%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lenin,_Trotsky_and_Voroshilov_with_Delegates_of_the_10th_Congress_of_the_Russian_Communist_Party_(Bolsheviks).jpg)

Trotsky with [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") and soldiers in Petrograd

Trotsky founded the [International Left Opposition](/wiki/International_Left_Opposition "International Left Opposition") in 1930. It was meant to be an opposition group within the [Comintern](/wiki/Comintern "Comintern"), but anyone who joined or was suspected of joining the ILO was immediately expelled from the Comintern. The ILO, therefore, concluded that opposing [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") from within the communist organizations controlled by Stalin's supporters had become impossible, so new organizations had to be formed. In 1933, the ILO was renamed the International Communist League (ICL), which formed the basis of the [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International"), founded in Paris in 1938.

Trotsky said that only the Fourth International, based on Lenin's theory of the vanguard party, could lead the world revolution and that it would need to be built in opposition to the capitalists and the Stalinists.

Trotsky argued that the defeat of the German working class and the coming to power of [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") in 1933 was due in part to the mistakes of the [Third Period](/wiki/Third_Period "Third Period") policy of the [Communist International](/wiki/Communist_International "Communist International") and that the subsequent failure of the Communist Parties to draw the correct lessons from those defeats showed that they were no longer capable of reform and a new international organisation of the working class must be organised. The [transitional demand](/wiki/Transitional_demand "Transitional demand") tactic had to be a key element.

At the time of the founding of the Fourth International in 1938, Trotskyism was a mass political current in [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") and slightly later [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia"). There was also a substantial Trotskyist movement in China which included the founding father of the Chinese communist movement, [Chen Duxiu](/wiki/Chen_Duxiu "Chen Duxiu"), amongst its number. Wherever Stalinists gained power, they prioritised hunting down Trotskyists and treated them as the worst enemies.[[151]](#cite_note-152)

The Fourth International suffered repression and disruption through the Second World War. Isolated from each other and faced with political developments quite unlike those anticipated by Trotsky, some Trotskyist organizations decided that the USSR could no longer be called a [degenerated workers' state](/wiki/Degenerated_workers%27_state "Degenerated workers' state") and withdrew from the Fourth International. After 1945, Trotskyism was smashed as a mass movement in Vietnam and marginalised in many other countries.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/V_A_Antonov-Ovseenko.jpeg/250px-V_A_Antonov-Ovseenko.jpeg)](/wiki/File:V_A_Antonov-Ovseenko.jpeg)

[Antonov-Ovseenko](/wiki/Vladimir_Antonov-Ovseenko "Vladimir Antonov-Ovseenko") was the first former Trotskyist to be posthumously [rehabilitated](/wiki/Rehabilitation_(Soviet) "Rehabilitation (Soviet)")

The [International Secretariat of the Fourth International](/wiki/International_Secretariat_of_the_Fourth_International "International Secretariat of the Fourth International") (ISFI) organised an international conference in 1946 and then World Congresses in 1948 and 1951 to assess the expropriation of the capitalists in Eastern Europe and Yugoslavia, the threat of a Third World War and the tasks of revolutionaries. The Eastern European Communist-led governments, which came into being after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") without a social revolution, were described by a resolution of the 1948 congress as presiding over capitalist economies.[[152]](#cite_note-153) By 1951, the Congress had concluded that they had become "deformed workers' states". As the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") intensified, the ISFI's 1951 World Congress adopted theses by [Michel Pablo](/wiki/Michel_Pablo "Michel Pablo") that anticipated an international civil war. Pablo's followers considered that the Communist Parties, under pressure from the real workers' movement, could escape Stalin's manipulations and follow a revolutionary orientation.

The 1951 Congress argued that Trotskyists should start to conduct systematic work inside those Communist Parties, followed by the majority of the working class. However, the ISFI's view that the Soviet leadership was counterrevolutionary remained unchanged. The 1951 Congress argued that the USSR took over these countries because of the military and political results of World War II and instituted nationalized property relations only after its attempts at placating capitalism failed to protect those countries from the threat of incursion by the West.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Ludwig_Binder_Haus_der_Geschichte_Studentenrevolte_1968_2001_03_0275.4212_%2817086177105%29.jpg/250px-Ludwig_Binder_Haus_der_Geschichte_Studentenrevolte_1968_2001_03_0275.4212_%2817086177105%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ludwig_Binder_Haus_der_Geschichte_Studentenrevolte_1968_2001_03_0275.4212_(17086177105).jpg)

The [West German student movement](/wiki/West_German_student_movement "West German student movement") in 1968

Pablo began expelling many people who disagreed with his thesis and did not want to dissolve their organizations within the Communist Parties. For instance, he expelled most of the French section and replaced its leadership. As a result, the opposition to Pablo eventually rose to the surface, with the Open Letter to Trotskyists of the World, by [Socialist Workers Party](/wiki/Socialist_Workers_Party_(United_States) "Socialist Workers Party (United States)") leader [James P. Cannon](/wiki/James_P._Cannon "James P. Cannon").

The Fourth International split in 1953 into two public factions. Several sections of the International established the [International Committee of the Fourth International](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Fourth_International "International Committee of the Fourth International") (ICFI) as an alternative centre to the International Secretariat, in which they felt a [revisionist](/wiki/Marxist_revisionism "Marxist revisionism") faction led by Michel Pablo had taken power and recommitted themselves to the Lenin-Trotsky Theory of the Party and Trotsky's theory of Permanent Revolution.[[153]](#cite_note-154) From 1960, led by the [U.S Socialist Workers Party](/wiki/Socialist_Workers_Party_(United_States) "Socialist Workers Party (United States)"), many ICFI sections began the reunification process with the IS, but factions split off and continued their commitment to the ICFI.[[154]](#cite_note-155)

### Cuban Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Cuban Revolution")]

Main article: [Betrayal thesis](/wiki/Betrayal_thesis "Betrayal thesis")

Historian [Theodore Draper](/wiki/Theodore_Draper "Theodore Draper") had been a Trotskyist in his youth, but eventually became a more independently minded historian, still skeptical of Stalinism. Draper originally gained scholarly recognition for his writings on the history of the American Communist Party.[[155]](#cite_note-156)

After the [Cuban Revolution](/wiki/Cuban_Revolution "Cuban Revolution"), a variety of socialist writers like [C. Wright Mills](/wiki/C._Wright_Mills "C. Wright Mills"), [Paul Sweezy](/wiki/Paul_Sweezy "Paul Sweezy"), [Herbert Matthews](/wiki/Herbert_Matthews "Herbert Matthews"), and [Paul Johnson](/wiki/Paul_Johnson_(writer) "Paul Johnson (writer)") were delivering polemics in defense of the new *Fidelista* government.[[156]](#cite_note-boys-157) In 1961 [Encounter magazine](/wiki/Encounter_(magazine) "Encounter (magazine)") asked [Theodore Draper](/wiki/Theodore_Draper "Theodore Draper") to write an assessment of the [Cuban Revolution](/wiki/Cuban_Revolution "Cuban Revolution") and the new provisional government. Draper published *Castro's Cuba - A Revolution Betrayed* in which, Draper presents a Marxist analysis which claims that "Castroism" is a rudimentary nationalism which ignores the principles of Marxism, and that Fidel Castro is a demagogue. Draper also describes his *[Betrayal thesis](/wiki/Betrayal_thesis "Betrayal thesis")* by alluding to Trotsky's allegation that Stalin "betrayed" the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution"). Draper alleges that Castro had "betrayed" the Cuban Revolution in a similar manner.[[156]](#cite_note-boys-157)[[157]](#cite_note-158)

Trotskyists in general, were split about Castroism. Trotskyists like [J. Posadas](/wiki/J._Posadas "J. Posadas") celebrated Castroism, and claimed that Cuba is on the road to socialism, while the British and French sections of the [International Committee of the Fourth International](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Fourth_International "International Committee of the Fourth International"), claimed that the Cuban Revolution only developed a form of state capitalism in Cuba.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

## Trotskyist movements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Trotskyist movements")]

See also: [List of Trotskyist organizations by country](/wiki/List_of_Trotskyist_organizations_by_country "List of Trotskyist organizations by country")

### Latin America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Latin America")]

Trotskyism has influenced some recent major social upheavals, particularly in Latin America. The [Bolivian Trotskyist party](/wiki/Revolutionary_Workers%27_Party_(Bolivia) "Revolutionary Workers' Party (Bolivia)") (*Partido Obrero Revolucionario*, POR) became a mass party in the late 1940s and early 1950s and, together with other groups, played a central role during and immediately after the period termed the [Bolivian National Revolution](/wiki/Bolivian_revolution#The_Bolivian_national_revolution_(1952–64) "Bolivian revolution").[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlexander1991-160)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Marcha_de_la_Resistencia_2017_24.jpg/250px-Marcha_de_la_Resistencia_2017_24.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marcha_de_la_Resistencia_2017_24.jpg)

[Workers' Left Front](/wiki/Workers%27_Left_Front "Workers' Left Front") in Argentina in December 2017

In Argentina, the [Workers' Revolutionary Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Revolutionary_Party_(Argentina) "Workers' Revolutionary Party (Argentina)") (Partido Revolucionario de los Trabajadores, PRT) lay in the merger of two leftist organizations in 1965, the [Revolutionary and Popular Amerindian Front](/w/index.php?title=Revolutionary_and_Popular_Amerindian_Front&action=edit&redlink=1 "Revolutionary and Popular Amerindian Front (page does not exist)") (*Frente Revolucionario Indoamericano Popular*, FRIP) and [Worker's Word](/w/index.php?title=Worker%27s_Word&action=edit&redlink=1 "Worker's Word (page does not exist)") *(Palabra Obrera, PO)*. In 1968, the PRT adhered to the [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International"), based in Paris. That same year, a related organisation was founded in Argentina, the ERP ([People's Revolutionary Army](/wiki/People%27s_Revolutionary_Army_(Argentina) "People's Revolutionary Army (Argentina)")), which became South America's most powerful rural guerrilla movement during the 1970s. The PRT left the Fourth International in 1973.[[160]](#cite_note-161) During the [Dirty War](/wiki/Dirty_War "Dirty War"), the Argentine military regime suppressed both the PRT and the ERP. ERP commander [Roberto Santucho](/wiki/Roberto_Santucho "Roberto Santucho") was killed in July 1976. Owing to the ruthless repression, PRT showed no signs of activity after 1977. During the 1980s in Argentina, the Trotskyist party founded in 1982 by [Nahuel Moreno](/wiki/Nahuel_Moreno "Nahuel Moreno"), MAS (*Movimiento al Socialismo*, [Movement for Socialism](/wiki/Movimiento_al_Socialismo_(Argentina) "Movimiento al Socialismo (Argentina)")), claimed to be the "largest Trotskyist party" in the world before it broke into many different fragments in the late 1980s, including the present-day [Workers' Socialist Movement](/wiki/Workers%27_Socialist_Movement_(Argentina) "Workers' Socialist Movement (Argentina)") (MST), [Socialist Workers' Party](/wiki/Socialist_Workers%27_Party_(Argentina) "Socialist Workers' Party (Argentina)") (PTS), [Socialist Left](/wiki/Socialist_Left_(Argentina) "Socialist Left (Argentina)") (IS), [Self-determination and Freedom](/wiki/Self-determination_and_Freedom "Self-determination and Freedom") (AyL, which is not outspoken Trotskyist). In 1989, an electoral front with the Communist Party and MRS called *Izquierda Unida* ("[United Left](/wiki/United_Left_(Argentina) "United Left (Argentina)")") retrieved 3.49% of the vote, representing 580,944 voters.[[161]](#cite_note-162) The [Workers' Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_(Argentina) "Workers' Party (Argentina)") (Partido Obrero) in Argentina later founded with other Trotskyist groups the [Workers' Left Front](/wiki/Workers%27_Left_Front "Workers' Left Front") which is represented in parliament.

Venezuelan president [Hugo Chávez](/wiki/Hugo_Ch%C3%A1vez "Hugo Chávez") declared himself a Trotskyist during the swearing-in of his cabinet two days before his inauguration on 10 January 2007.[[162]](#cite_note-163) Venezuelan Trotskyist organizations do not regard Chávez as a Trotskyist, with some describing him as a bourgeois nationalist.[[163]](#cite_note-164) In contrast, others consider him an honest revolutionary leader who made significant mistakes due to his lack of a Marxist analysis.[[164]](#cite_note-165)

### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: North America")]

> They fear, in a word, that [Soviet America](/wiki/Socialism_in_the_United_States "Socialism in the United States") will become the counterpart of what they have been told Soviet Russia looks like. Actually American soviets will be as different from the Russian soviets as the United States of President Roosevelt differs from the Russian Empire of Czar Nicholas II. Yet communism can come in America only through revolution, just as independence and democracy came in America.

Trotsky on *If America Should Go Communist* in 1934.[[165]](#cite_note-166)

The development of the American Trotskyism movement emerged with the [Communist League of America (CLA)](/wiki/Communist_League_of_America "Communist League of America"), then as the [Workers Party of the United States (WPUS)](/wiki/Workers_Party_of_the_United_States "Workers Party of the United States") and briefly as the [Socialist Party (SP) of America](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_America "Socialist Party of America") before beginning in 1938 as the [Socialist Workers Party (SWP)](/wiki/Socialist_Workers_Party_(United_States) "Socialist Workers Party (United States)").[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlancWaldBreitman201639-167) According to historian Paul Le Blanc, [James Cannon](/wiki/James_P._Cannon "James P. Cannon") and [Max Shachtman](/wiki/Max_Shachtman "Max Shachtman") were the most influential leaders of early US Trotskyism.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlancWaldBreitman201654-168)

Trotsky engaged with members of the [American Socialist Workers Party](/wiki/American_Socialist_Workers_Party "American Socialist Workers Party") on reaching the Black population. He had correspondence with [C.L.R. James](/wiki/C.L.R._James "C.L.R. James") on the question of [self-determination](/wiki/African-American_self-determination "African-American self-determination") and expressed support for Black Americans seeking equal rights and an autonomous state.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirson1992184–190-169)

In 1953 there was a major split in the Fourth International between the [International Secretariat of the Fourth International (ISFI)](/wiki/International_Secretariat_of_the_Fourth_International "International Secretariat of the Fourth International") led by [Michel Pablo](/wiki/Michel_Pablo "Michel Pablo") and the [International Committee of the Fourth International (ICFI)](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Fourth_International "International Committee of the Fourth International") led iniatially by [James Cannon](/wiki/James_P._Cannon "James P. Cannon") of the [SWP](/wiki/Socialist_Workers_Party_(United_States) "Socialist Workers Party (United States)"). While a decade later in 1963 the majority of the SWP reunited with Pablo's supporters, a minority group persisted in their independence and criticisms of what they deemed to be the ISFI's abandonment of Permanent Revolution and their promotion of Stalinst regimes and parties. This group that remained loyal to the ICFI would go on to become the [Socialist Equality Party (US)](/wiki/Socialist_Equality_Party_(US) "Socialist Equality Party (US)").[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171) There is also another North American national section of the ICFI in Canada, also known as the Socialist Equality Party.

There is a Trotskyist-influenced caucus within the [Democratic Socialists of America](/wiki/Democratic_Socialists_of_America "Democratic Socialists of America"), Reform and Revolution.[[171]](#cite_note-172)

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Africa")]

Through the 1930s, the first viable black trade unions in [Transvaal](/wiki/Transvaal_(province) "Transvaal (province)"), South Africa were established by Trotskyists.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirson1992177–181-173)

The [Democratic Socialist Movement (Nigeria)](/wiki/Democratic_Socialist_Movement_(Nigeria) "Democratic Socialist Movement (Nigeria)") exists in [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), it was founded in 1986 among a confederation of labour and student socialists. It is affiliated to the [Committee for a Workers' International](/wiki/Committee_for_a_Workers%27_International_(2019) "Committee for a Workers' International (2019)").

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Asia")]

#### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: China")]

In China, various left opposition groups in the late 1920s sought to engage Trotsky against the Comintern policy of support for the Kuomintang.[[173]](#cite_note-174) In 1931, at Trotsky's urging, the various factions united in the Communist League of China, adopting Trotsky's document "The Political Situation in China and the Task of the Bolshevik-Leninist Opposition". Prominent members include [Chen Duxiu](/wiki/Chen_Duxiu "Chen Duxiu"), [Wang Fanxi](/wiki/Wang_Fanxi "Wang Fanxi") and [Chen Qichang](/wiki/Chen_Qichang_(Trotskyist) "Chen Qichang (Trotskyist)"). The League was persecuted by the Nationalist government and by the Chinese Communist Party.[[174]](#cite_note-175)

In 1949, the [Revolutionary Communist Party of China](/wiki/Revolutionary_Communist_Party_of_China "Revolutionary Communist Party of China") ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 中國革命共產黨; RCP) fled to Hong Kong. Since 1974, the party has been legally active as October Review, its official publication.[[175]](#cite_note-176)

#### Vietnam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Vietnam")]

In French Indochina during the 1930s, [Trotskyism in Vietnam](/wiki/Trotskyism_in_Vietnam "Trotskyism in Vietnam"), led by [Tạ Thu Thâu](/wiki/T%E1%BA%A1_Thu_Th%C3%A2u "Tạ Thu Thâu"), was a significant current, particularly in [Saigon](/wiki/Saigon "Saigon"), Cochinchina.[[176]](#cite_note-177) In 1929, in the French Left Opposition *La Vérité*, Ta Thu Thau condemned the Comintern for leading Chinese Communists (in 1927) to "the graveyard" through its support for the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang"). The "'[Sun Yat-sen](/wiki/Sun_Yat-sen "Sun Yat-sen")-ist' synthesis of democracy, nationalism and socialism" was "a kind of nationalist mysticism". In Indochina, it could only obscure "the concrete class relationships, and the real, organic liaison between the indigenous bourgeoisie and French imperialism," in the light of which the call for independence is "mechanical and formalistic". "A revolution based on the organisation of the proletarian and peasant masses is the only one capable of liberating the colonies ... The question of independence must be bound up with that of the proletarian socialist revolution."[[177]](#cite_note-178)

For a period in the 1930s, Ta Thu Thau's Struggle group, centred around the newspaper [La Lutte](/wiki/La_Lutte_(newspaper) "La Lutte (newspaper)"), was sufficiently strong to induce "Stalinists" (members of the [Indochinese Communist Party](/wiki/Indochinese_Communist_Party "Indochinese Communist Party")) to collaborate with the Trotskyists in support of labour and peasant struggles, and in the presentation of a common Workers Slate for Saigon municipal, and Cochinchina Council, elections. Ta Thu Thau was captured and executed by the Stalinist-front [Viet Minh](/wiki/Viet_Minh "Viet Minh") in September 1945. Many, if not most, of his fellow *luttuers* were subsequently killed, caught between the Viet Minh and the French effort at colonial reconquest.[[178]](#cite_note-179)

#### Sri Lanka

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Sri Lanka")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Lsspoffice.JPG/250px-Lsspoffice.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lsspoffice.JPG)

[LSSP](/wiki/Lanka_Sama_Samaja_Party "Lanka Sama Samaja Party") main office in [Colombo](/wiki/Colombo "Colombo"), Sri Lanka

In Sri Lanka, a group of Trotskyists (known as the "T Group"), including South Asia's pioneer Trotskyist, [Philip Gunawardena](/wiki/Philip_Gunawardena "Philip Gunawardena"), who had been active in Trotskyist politics in Europe, and his colleague [N. M. Perera](/wiki/N._M._Perera "N. M. Perera"), were instrumental in the foundation of the [Lanka Sama Samaja Party](/wiki/Lanka_Sama_Samaja_Party "Lanka Sama Samaja Party") (LSSP) in 1935. It expelled its pro-Moscow wing in 1940, becoming a Trotskyist-led party. After a prison break it helped form the short lived [Bolshevik–Leninist Party of India, Ceylon and Burma](/wiki/Bolshevik%E2%80%93Leninist_Party_of_India,_Ceylon_and_Burma "Bolshevik–Leninist Party of India, Ceylon and Burma") (BLPI).

After the war, the Sri Lanka section split into the Lanka Sama Samaja Party and the [Bolshevik Samasamaja Party](/wiki/Bolshevik_Samasamaja_Party "Bolshevik Samasamaja Party") (BSP). In the general election of 1947, the LSSP became the main opposition party, winning ten seats, the BSP winning a further 5. It joined the Trotskyist Fourth International after fusion with the BSP in 1950 and led a general strike ([Hartal](/wiki/Hartal_1953 "Hartal 1953")) in 1953.[[179]](#cite_note-Ervin-180)[[180]](#cite_note-181)[[181]](#cite_note-182)

In 1964 a faction of the LSSP broke away in response to the party joining the governing coalition of [Sirimavo Bandaranaike](/wiki/Sirimavo_Bandaranaike "Sirimavo Bandaranaike") and affiliated itself with the [International Committee of the Fourth International](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Fourth_International "International Committee of the Fourth International") who shared their critiques of Trotskyist parties joining bourgeois governments. In 1968 they would officially form the Revolutionary Communist League which would eventually become the Socialist Equality Party in 1996.[[182]](#cite_note-183)

In 1974, a secret faction of the LSSP, allied to the [Militant](/wiki/Militant_(Trotskyist_group) "Militant (Trotskyist group)") group in the United Kingdom, emerged. In 1977, this faction was expelled and formed the [Nava Sama Samaja Party](/wiki/Nava_Sama_Samaja_Party "Nava Sama Samaja Party"), led by [Vasudeva Nanayakkara](/wiki/Vasudeva_Nanayakkara "Vasudeva Nanayakkara").

#### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: India")]

In 1942, following the escape of the leaders of the Sri Lankan LSSP from a [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") prison, a unified [Bolshevik–Leninist Party of India, Ceylon and Burma](/wiki/Bolshevik%E2%80%93Leninist_Party_of_India,_Ceylon_and_Burma "Bolshevik–Leninist Party of India, Ceylon and Burma") (BLPI) was established in India, bringing together the many Trotskyist groups in the subcontinent. The BLPI was active in the [Quit India Movement](/wiki/Quit_India_Movement "Quit India Movement") and the labour movement, capturing the second oldest union in India. Its high point was when it led the strikes which followed the [Bombay Mutiny](/wiki/Bombay_Mutiny "Bombay Mutiny").

In India, the BLPI fractured. In 1948, at the Fourth International's request, the party's rump dissolved into the [Congress Socialist Party](/wiki/Congress_Socialist_Party "Congress Socialist Party") as an exercise in [entryism](/wiki/Entryism "Entryism").[[183]](#cite_note-184)[[179]](#cite_note-Ervin-180)

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Europe")]

#### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Spain")]

In the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), Stalinist-directed NKVD oversaw purges of anti-Stalinist elements in the Spanish [Republican](/wiki/Spanish_Republican "Spanish Republican") forces including Trotskyist and [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchist "Anarchist") factions.[[184]](#cite_note-185) Notable cases involved the execution of [Andreu Nin](/wiki/Andreu_Nin "Andreu Nin"), former government minister in [Revolutionary Catalonia](/wiki/Revolutionary_Catalonia "Revolutionary Catalonia"), [Jose Robles](/wiki/Jose_Robles "Jose Robles"), a left-wing academic and translator along with many members of the Trotskyist-aligned [POUM](/wiki/POUM "POUM") faction, such as [Mary Stanley Low](/wiki/Mary_Stanley_Low "Mary Stanley Low").[[185]](#cite_note-186)[[186]](#cite_note-187)[[187]](#cite_note-188)[[188]](#cite_note-189)

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

In Britain during the 1980s, the [entryist](/wiki/Entryism "Entryism") [Militant](/wiki/Militant_(Trotskyist_group) "Militant (Trotskyist group)") group operated within the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") with three members of parliament and effective control of [Liverpool City Council](/wiki/Liverpool_City_Council "Liverpool City Council"), and was described by journalist [Michael Crick](/wiki/Michael_Crick "Michael Crick") as "Britain's fifth most important political party" in 1986.[[189]](#cite_note-190)

One of the more prominent Trotskyist parties in Britain has been the [Socialist Workers Party](/wiki/Socialist_Workers_Party_(UK) "Socialist Workers Party (UK)"), formerly the International Socialists (IS) and traces its history back to 1950. The SWP has helped found several organisations through which they have sought to exert influence over the broader left, such as the [Anti-Nazi League](/wiki/Anti-Nazi_League "Anti-Nazi League") in the late 1970s and the [Stop the War Coalition](/wiki/Stop_the_War_Coalition "Stop the War Coalition") in 2001.[[190]](#cite_note-191) It also allied with [George Galloway](/wiki/George_Galloway "George Galloway") and [Respect](/wiki/Respect_Party "Respect Party"), whose dissolution in 2007 caused an internal crisis in the SWP. A more serious internal crisis, leading to a significant decline in the party's membership, emerged in 2013 over allegations of rape and sexual assault made against a leading party member.[[191]](#cite_note-192)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Dungavel3_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Dungavel3_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dungavel3_(cropped).JPG)

Scottish TUSC members protesting against the [Dungavel Detention Centre](/wiki/Dungavel_Immigration_Removal_Centre "Dungavel Immigration Removal Centre")

The [Trade Unionist and Socialist Coalition](/wiki/Trade_Unionist_and_Socialist_Coalition "Trade Unionist and Socialist Coalition") (TUSC) was formed in 2010 between the [Socialist Party](/wiki/Socialist_Party_(England_and_Wales) "Socialist Party (England and Wales)"), the SWP and [Socialist Resistance](/wiki/Socialist_Resistance "Socialist Resistance"), along with the [RMT](/wiki/National_Union_of_Rail,_Maritime_and_Transport_Workers "National Union of Rail, Maritime and Transport Workers") union, has participated in several general elections.[[192]](#cite_note-193) The TUSC stood 40 candidates at the [2024 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election").[[193]](#cite_note-194)

In April 2019, a 1970s splinter from IS made headlines when three former members of the [Revolutionary Communist Party](/wiki/Revolutionary_Communist_Party_(UK,_1978) "Revolutionary Communist Party (UK, 1978)") campaigned in the European Parliamentary election as candidates for the [Brexit Party](/wiki/Brexit_Party "Brexit Party"),[[194]](#cite_note-Walker,_2019-195)[[195]](#cite_note-196)[[196]](#cite_note-197) and a fourth, [Munira Mirza](/wiki/Munira_Mirza "Munira Mirza"), was appointed head of the Number 10 [Downing Street](/wiki/Downing_Street "Downing Street") policy unit by the new Conservative Prime Minister [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson").[[197]](#cite_note-198) The RCP's rejection of the SWP's critical engagement with the Labour Party and trade unions had morphed into embracing right-wing libertarian positions.[[194]](#cite_note-Walker,_2019-195)

#### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Ireland")]

The [Socialist Party](/wiki/Socialist_Party_(Ireland) "Socialist Party (Ireland)") in Ireland was formed in 1996 by members who had been expelled from the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(Ireland) "Labour Party (Ireland)") in 1989 under the leadership of [Dick Spring](/wiki/Dick_Spring "Dick Spring"). It achieved electoral success at the [1997 general election](/wiki/1997_Irish_general_election "1997 Irish general election") with the election of [Joe Higgins](/wiki/Joe_Higgins_(politician) "Joe Higgins (politician)") in [Dublin West](/wiki/Dublin_West "Dublin West"). The Socialist Party has been part of electoral alliances such as the [United Left Alliance](/wiki/United_Left_Alliance "United Left Alliance"), [Solidarity](/wiki/Solidarity_(Ireland) "Solidarity (Ireland)") and [People Before Profit–Solidarity](/wiki/People_Before_Profit%E2%80%93Solidarity "People Before Profit–Solidarity"). As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit), it is represented at a national level by [Mick Barry](/wiki/Mick_Barry_(Irish_politician) "Mick Barry (Irish politician)"), a TD for [Cork North-Central](/wiki/Cork_North-Central "Cork North-Central"). It contests [elections in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Elections_in_Northern_Ireland "Elections in Northern Ireland") as part of the [Cross-Community Labour Alternative](/wiki/Cross-Community_Labour_Alternative "Cross-Community Labour Alternative").

#### Portugal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Portugal")]

In Portugal's [October 2015 parliamentary election](/wiki/2015_Portuguese_legislative_election "2015 Portuguese legislative election"), the [Left Bloc](/wiki/Left_Bloc_(Portugal) "Left Bloc (Portugal)") won 550,945 votes, translating into 10.19% of the expressed votes and 19 (out of 230) *deputados* (members of parliament).[[198]](#cite_note-199) Although founded by several leftist tendencies, it still expresses much of the Trotskyist thought upheld and developed by its former leader, [Francisco Louçã](/wiki/Francisco_Lou%C3%A7%C3%A3 "Francisco Louçã").

#### Turkey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Turkey")]

In [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), there are some Trotskyist organizations, including the [International Socialist Tendency](/wiki/International_Socialist_Tendency "International Socialist Tendency")'s section ([Revolutionary Socialist Workers' Party](/wiki/Revolutionary_Socialist_Workers%27_Party_(Turkey) "Revolutionary Socialist Workers' Party (Turkey)")). The [International Committee of the Fourth International](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Fourth_International "International Committee of the Fourth International")'s Turkish section, the Socialist Equality Party (formerly the Socialist Equality Group), have held several public meetings on the history of the Trotskyist movement on [Büyükada](/wiki/B%C3%BCy%C3%BCkada "Büyükada"), also known as Prinkipo, an island in the [Sea of Marmara](/wiki/Sea_of_Marmara "Sea of Marmara") that served as Trotsky's first exiled home from 1929 to 1933 after being deported from the USSR.[[199]](#cite_note-200)[[200]](#cite_note-201)[[201]](#cite_note-202)

#### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Germany")]

A section of the [International Committee of the Fourth International](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Fourth_International "International Committee of the Fourth International") has existed in Germany since 1971, originally founded as the Federation of Socialist Workers (*Bund Sozialistischer Arbeiter*), it adopted its current name [Socialist Equality Party](/wiki/Socialist_Equality_Party_(Germany) "Socialist Equality Party (Germany)") (*Sozialistische Gleichheitspartei*) in 2017.

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: France")]

Main article: [Trotskyism in France](/wiki/Trotskyism_in_France "Trotskyism in France")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Manif_Paris_2005-11-19_dsc06344_cropped.jpg/250px-Manif_Paris_2005-11-19_dsc06344_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manif_Paris_2005-11-19_dsc06344_cropped.jpg)

[LCR](/wiki/Revolutionary_Communist_League_(France) "Revolutionary Communist League (France)") protesters marching in a workforce demonstration in favour of public services and against privatization

The [Communist League](/wiki/Communist_League_(1930) "Communist League (1930)") was formed in 1930, becoming the first Trotskyist group in the country.[[202]](#cite_note-203) French Trotskyists have historically faced internal splits and external repression, notably during World War II when their activities were banned.[[203]](#cite_note-204)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Postwar, French Trotskyism was shaped by significant divisions, such as the 1952 split between [Lambertist](/wiki/Lambertist "Lambertist") and [Pabloist](/wiki/Pabloist "Pabloist") factions, reflecting global tensions within the [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International").[[204]](#cite_note-205)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The movement gained public attention during events like the [May 1968 protests](/wiki/May_1968_protests "May 1968 protests"), where Trotskyist groups, including the [Revolutionary Communist League](/wiki/Revolutionary_Communist_League_(France) "Revolutionary Communist League (France)") (LCR), played a prominent role.[[205]](#cite_note-206)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

The French section of the Fourth International was the Internationalist Communist Party (PCI). In 1952 the party split when the Fourth International removed its Central Committee and split again when in 1953, the Fourth International itself divided. Further divisions occurred over which independence faction to support in the Algerian War. In 1967, the rump of the PCI renamed itself the "[Internationalist Communist Organisation](/wiki/Internationalist_Communist_Organisation "Internationalist Communist Organisation")" (*Organisation Communiste Internationaliste*, OCI). It proliferated during the May 1968 student demonstrations but was banned alongside other far-left groups, such as the *Gauche prolétarienne* (Proletarian Left). Members temporarily reconstituted the group as the Trotskyist Organisation but soon obtained a state order permitting the reformation of the OCI. By 1970, the OCI was able to organise a 10,000-strong youth rally. The group also gained a strong base in trade unions. However, further splits and disintegration followed.

Today, France remains a hub for Trotskyist activity, represented by groups such as *[Lutte Ouvrière](/wiki/Lutte_Ouvri%C3%A8re "Lutte Ouvrière")*. In [2002](/wiki/2002_French_presidential_election "2002 French presidential election"), three trotskyist candidates ran in the election. [Arlette Laguiller](/wiki/Arlette_Laguiller "Arlette Laguiller") of *Lutte Ouvrière* got 5.72%, [Olivier Besancenot](/wiki/Olivier_Besancenot "Olivier Besancenot") of the [Revolutionary Communist League](/wiki/Revolutionary_Communist_League_(France) "Revolutionary Communist League (France)") (*Ligue communiste révolutionnaire*) got 4.25% and [Daniel Gluckstein](/wiki/Daniel_Gluckstein "Daniel Gluckstein") of the [Workers' Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_(France) "Workers' Party (France)") (*Parti des Travailleurs*) got 0.47%.[[206]](#cite_note-207) In 2016 [Jean-Luc Mélenchon](/wiki/Jean-Luc_M%C3%A9lenchon "Jean-Luc Mélenchon"), formerly of the ICO, launched the left-wing political platform *[La France Insoumise](/wiki/La_France_Insoumise "La France Insoumise")* (Unbowed France), subsequently endorsed by several parties, including his own [Left Party](/wiki/Left_Party_(France) "Left Party (France)") and the [French Communist Party](/wiki/French_Communist_Party "French Communist Party"). In the 2017 French Presidential Election, he received 19% in the first round. In the same election, [Philippe Poutou](/wiki/Philippe_Poutou "Philippe Poutou") of the [New Anticapitalist Party](/wiki/New_Anticapitalist_Party "New Anticapitalist Party"), into which the Revolutionary Communist League dissolved itself in 2008, won 1.20% of the vote. The only openly Trotskyist candidate, [Nathalie Arthaud](/wiki/Nathalie_Arthaud "Nathalie Arthaud") of Workers' Struggle, won 0.64% of the vote.

## Internationals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Internationals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Socialist_Alternative_Protest1.jpg/250px-Socialist_Alternative_Protest1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Socialist_Alternative_Protest1.jpg)

[Socialist Alternative](/wiki/Socialist_Alternative_(United_States) "Socialist Alternative (United States)") members in the United States at an antiwar march in 2007

The [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International_(Post-Reunification) "Fourth International (Post-Reunification)") derives from the 1963 reunification of the two public factions into which the Fourth International split in 1953: the [International Secretariat of the Fourth International](/wiki/International_Secretariat_of_the_Fourth_International "International Secretariat of the Fourth International") (ISFI) and some sections of the [International Committee of the Fourth International](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Fourth_International "International Committee of the Fourth International") (ICFI). It is often referred to as the United Secretariat of the Fourth International, the name of its leading committee before 2003. The USFI retains sections and sympathizing organizations in over 50 countries, including France's *[Ligue Communiste Revolutionnaire](/wiki/Ligue_Communiste_Revolutionnaire "Ligue Communiste Revolutionnaire")* (LCR) and sections in Portugal, Sri Lanka, the Philippines, and Pakistan.[[207]](#cite_note-208)

The [International Committee of the Fourth International](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Fourth_International "International Committee of the Fourth International") maintains its independent organization and publishes the [World Socialist Web Site](/wiki/World_Socialist_Web_Site "World Socialist Web Site"). They have full sections in the US, UK, Canada, Germany, France, Sri Lanka, Turkey, and Australia with sympathizing groups in Russia, Ukraine, New Zealand, India, Pakistan, Brazil, Croatia, Ireland, and other countries.

The [Committee for a Workers' International](/wiki/Committee_for_a_Workers%27_International_(1974) "Committee for a Workers' International (1974)") (CWI) was founded in 1974 and has sections in over 35 countries. Before 1997, most organisations affiliated with the CWI sought to build an entrist Marxist wing within the large [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democratic "Social democratic") parties. The CWI has adopted a range of tactics, including working with trade unions, but in some cases working within or supporting other parties, endorsing [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") for the [2016 U.S. Democratic Party nomination](/wiki/Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries,_2016 "Democratic Party presidential primaries, 2016") and encouraging him to run independently.[[208]](#cite_note-209)

In France, the LCR is rivalled by *[Lutte Ouvrière](/wiki/Lutte_Ouvri%C3%A8re "Lutte Ouvrière")*, the French section of the [Internationalist Communist Union](/wiki/Internationalist_Communist_Union "Internationalist Communist Union") (UCI), with small sections in a handful of other countries. It focuses its activities, whether propaganda or intervention, on the industrial proletariat.

The Committee for a Marxist International (CMI) founders claims they were expelled from the CWI when the CWI abandoned [entryism](/wiki/Entryism "Entryism"). The CWI claims they left, and no expulsions were carried out. In 2006, it became the International Marxist Tendency (IMT). CMI/IMT groups continue the policy of entering mainstream social democratic, communist or radical parties. Known as the [Revolutionary Communist International](/wiki/Revolutionary_Communist_International "Revolutionary Communist International") (RCI) since 2024, it is headed by [Alan Woods](/wiki/Alan_Woods_(politician) "Alan Woods (politician)").

The [list of Trotskyist internationals](/wiki/List_of_Trotskyist_internationals "List of Trotskyist internationals") shows that there are a large number of other multinational tendencies that stand in the tradition of Leon Trotsky.

## Debated comparison with Stalinism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Debated comparison with Stalinism")]

Main articles: [Anti-Stalinist Left](/wiki/Anti-Stalinist_Left "Anti-Stalinist Left"), [The Declaration of 46](/wiki/The_Declaration_of_46 "The Declaration of 46"), [New Course (Trotsky book)](/wiki/New_Course_(Trotsky_book) "New Course (Trotsky book)"), [The Stalin School of Falsification](/wiki/The_Stalin_School_of_Falsification "The Stalin School of Falsification"), and [Stalin: An Appraisal of the Man and His Influence](/wiki/Stalin:_An_Appraisal_of_the_Man_and_His_Influence "Stalin: An Appraisal of the Man and His Influence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Joseph_Stalin_and_Leon_Trotsky_at_Felix_Dzerzhinsky_funeral.jpg/250px-Joseph_Stalin_and_Leon_Trotsky_at_Felix_Dzerzhinsky_funeral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph_Stalin_and_Leon_Trotsky_at_Felix_Dzerzhinsky_funeral.jpg)

[Kalinin](/wiki/Mikhail_Kalinin "Mikhail Kalinin") and [Stalin](/wiki/Stalin "Stalin") bearing the coffin of [Felix Dzerzhinsky](/wiki/Felix_Dzerzhinsky "Felix Dzerzhinsky") on 22 July 1926. Trotsky can be seen over Kalinin's left shoulder.

Some Western historians have regarded Trotsky as a forerunner to Stalinism and centred this notion on his record during the period of [war communism](/wiki/War_communism "War communism"), which included practices such as the [militarization of labour](/wiki/Labor_army "Labor army").[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels2008194–195-210) Criticism has also been levied at his support for [concentration camps](/wiki/Concentration_camps "Concentration camps") to detain [war prisoners](/wiki/War_prisoners "War prisoners") and the [Red Terror](/wiki/Red_Terror "Red Terror").[[210]](#cite_note-auto2-211)[[11]](#cite_note-Taylor_&_Francis-11) Other historians such as [Robert Service](/wiki/Robert_Service_(historian) "Robert Service (historian)"), [Dmitri Volkogonov](/wiki/Dmitri_Volkogonov "Dmitri Volkogonov"), N.A. Vasetskii and Alter Litvin contend that Trotsky shared the same [totalitarian](/wiki/Totalitarian "Totalitarian") strand of thought as Stalin and would not have represented a radically different USSR.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThatcher2005[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidcU3yFMLm1voCqvolkogonovpgPT91_1–30]-212)[[212]](#cite_note-213)[[213]](#cite_note-214) Concerning the ideological differences between the varieties of [Marxist philosophy](/wiki/Marxist_philosophy "Marxist philosophy") that are Stalinism and Trotskyism, novelist [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell") said:

> The fact that Trotskyists are everywhere a persecuted minority, and that the accusation usually made against them, i.e. of collaborating with the [Fascists](/wiki/Fascist "Fascist"), is obviously false, creates an impression that Trotskyism is intellectually and morally superior to Communism; but it is doubtful whether there is much difference.[[214]](#cite_note-215)

However, literary critic [Jeffrey Meyers](/wiki/Jeffrey_Meyers "Jeffrey Meyers"), who reviewed the political allegories in Orwell's work, stated that:

> "Orwell ignores the fact that Trotsky passionately opposed [Stalin’s dictatorship from 1924 to 1940](/wiki/Left_Opposition "Left Opposition"), which featured [Siberian](/wiki/Siberian "Siberian") prison camps, the deliberately created [Ukraine famine](/wiki/Ukraine_famine "Ukraine famine") and the massive slaughter during the [Moscow Purge Trials of 1937](/wiki/Great_Purges "Great Purges").”[[215]](#cite_note-216)

> “With all the greater frankness can I state how, in my view, the Soviet government should act in case of a fascist upheaval in Germany. In their place, I would, at the very moment of receiving telegraphic news of this event, sign a [mobilisation](/wiki/Mobilisation "Mobilisation") order calling up several age groups. In the face of a mortal enemy, when the logic of the situation points to inevitable war, it would be irresponsible and unpardonable to give that enemy time to establish himself, to consolidate his positions, to conclude alliances… and to work out the plan to attack.”

Trotsky describing the military measures he would have taken in place of Stalin to counter the rise of [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") in 1932.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher20151192–1193-217)

Meyers further added that Orwell reinforced his arguments by using the views of a [right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing "Right-wing") combatant. In contrast, Meyers cited [Isaac Deutscher](/wiki/Isaac_Deutscher "Isaac Deutscher")'s biographical account of Trotsky, which presented him to be a much more civilised figure than Stalin and suggested that he would not have [purged the Red Army generals](/wiki/Case_of_the_Trotskyist_Anti-Soviet_Military_Organization "Case of the Trotskyist Anti-Soviet Military Organization") or millions of Soviet citizens.[[217]](#cite_note-218)

Historian [Sheila Fitzpatrick](/wiki/Sheila_Fitzpatrick "Sheila Fitzpatrick") has also questioned the premise of historical inevitability presented by [conservative](/wiki/Conservative "Conservative") critics such as [Robert Service](/wiki/Robert_Service_(historian) "Robert Service (historian)") in that the Soviet Union would have experienced the same "totalitarian [despotism](/wiki/Despotism "Despotism") under Trotskyist rule". Fitzpatrick suggested it was implausible that Trotsky, like Stalin, would have launched an [anti-semitic campaign](/wiki/Anti-cosmopolitan_campaign "Anti-cosmopolitan campaign") after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") or initiated the [Great Purges](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge"). Rather, she inferred that Trotsky would presumably have provided good [leadership](/wiki/Leadership "Leadership") during the Second World War but may have struggled to maintain party cohesion as seen during the succession struggle after 1924.[[218]](#cite_note-219)

Moreover, several scholars such as Richard B.Day, [Vadim Rogovin](/wiki/Vadim_Rogovin "Vadim Rogovin") and [Robert Vincent Daniels](/wiki/Robert_Vincent_Daniels "Robert Vincent Daniels") along with Western [socialists](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") have considered Trotsky to have represented a more [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") [alternative](/wiki/Anti-Stalinist_Left "Anti-Stalinist Left") to Stalin.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels2008189–198-220)[[220]](#cite_note-221)[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogovin20211–15,_497-222)[[222]](#cite_note-223)[[223]](#cite_note-224) In particular, emphasis has been drawn to his activities in the pre-Civil War period and as leader of the Left Opposition.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels2008189–198-220) Proponents of this view have specified further differences with Stalinism, which emerged during the succession struggle, over [intraparty democracy](/wiki/Intragroup_conflict "Intragroup conflict"), autonomy of the [Comintern](/wiki/Comintern "Comintern") and the [dogmatization of Leninist orthodoxy](/wiki/Cult_of_personality#Soviet_Union "Cult of personality").[[224]](#cite_note-225) Mandel and Deutscher maintain that his intra-party reforms from 1923 to 1926 would have revitalised party democratization, [mass participation](/wiki/Public_participation "Public participation"), [worker's self-management](/wiki/Worker%27s_self-management "Worker's self-management") and eventually a [multi-party](/wiki/Multi-party "Multi-party") [socialist democracy](/wiki/Socialist_democracy "Socialist democracy").[[225]](#cite_note-226)[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015674–678,_826-227) Trotsky also opposed the policy of [forced collectivisation](/wiki/Collectivization_in_the_Soviet_Union "Collectivization in the Soviet Union") under Stalin and favoured a [voluntary](/wiki/Volunteering "Volunteering"), gradual approach towards [agricultural production](/wiki/Collective_farming "Collective farming"),[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeilharz1987171-228)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERubenstein2011[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleontrotskyrevol0000rubepage160mode2upqforcedcollectivization_161]-17) with greater tolerance for the rights of Soviet Ukrainians.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015637-229)[[229]](#cite_note-230) Daniels viewed Trotsky and the Left Opposition as a [critical alternative](/wiki/Anti-Stalinist_Left "Anti-Stalinist Left") to the Stalin-[Bukharin](/wiki/Bukharin "Bukharin") majority in a number of areas. He also stated that the Left Opposition would have prioritised [industrialisation](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") but never contemplated the "[violent uprooting](/wiki/Collectivization_in_the_Soviet_Union "Collectivization in the Soviet Union")" employed by Stalin and contrasted most directly with Stalinism on the issue of [party democratization and bureaucratization](/wiki/Soviet_democracy "Soviet democracy").[[230]](#cite_note-231)

Other figures such as [Baruch Knei-Paz](/wiki/The_Social_and_Political_Thought_of_Leon_Trotsky "The Social and Political Thought of Leon Trotsky") and [Victor Serge](/wiki/Victor_Serge "Victor Serge") acknowledge some affinities with Stalinism in regards to the use of coercion but have also recognised clear differences between Trotsky and Stalin.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978298–299-232)[[232]](#cite_note-233) On the [cultural field](/wiki/Culture "Culture"), Knei-Paz highlighted their contrasting attitudes towards matters such as the [arts](/wiki/Arts "Arts") and [sciences](/wiki/Sciences "Sciences").[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978289–301-85) According to Knei-Paz, it does not seem [credible](/wiki/Credibility "Credibility") that Trotsky would have treated [culture and society](/wiki/Literature_and_Revolution "Literature and Revolution") with the same total, brutal disregard as Stalin.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978286,_289–301-234) He also argued that Trotsky sought [far-reaching economic, commercial relations with European countries](/wiki/Towards_Socialism_or_Capitalism%3F "Towards Socialism or Capitalism?") which was at variance with the [economic policy](/wiki/Economic_policy "Economic policy") of [isolationism](/wiki/Isolationism "Isolationism") and harsher measures pursued under Stalin.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978289–301-85) In the view of Knei-Paz, the most discernible difference between Trotsky and Stalin was their respective approaches towards [international affairs](/wiki/Proletarian_internationalism "Proletarian internationalism").[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978300–301-235) In this matter, Trotsky firmly adhered to the notion that socialism in Russia, as “[backward society](/wiki/Underdevelopment "Underdevelopment")” was dependent on revolution in the West.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978300–301-235) Biographer Geoffrey Swain believed that the Soviet Union under the leadership of Trotsky would have been more [technocratic](/wiki/Technocratic "Technocratic") as he would have made far more use of "[bourgeois experts](/wiki/Technicians "Technicians")" in the [planning process](/wiki/Economic_planning "Economic planning"), and inferred this from his conduct during the Civil War along with his writings in the early 1920s.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwain2014[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid_a3pAgAAQBAJdqtrotsky27ssovietunionwouldhavebeenfarmoretechnocraticpgPA118_118]-119) Swain also expressed the view that the Soviet Union under Trotsky would certainly have been [a less terrorised society](/wiki/Excess_mortality_in_the_Soviet_Union_under_Joseph_Stalin "Excess mortality in the Soviet Union under Joseph Stalin") yet was critical of his military methods [in relation to desertion](/wiki/Barrier_troops "Barrier troops") and hostage taking during the Civil War.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwain2014[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidP-ahAwAAQBAJdqtrotskylessterrorisedsocietypgPA3_3,_211]-236)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Diego_rivera_Commies.jpg/250px-Diego_rivera_Commies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Diego_rivera_Commies.jpg)

A [Diego Rivera](/wiki/Diego_Rivera "Diego Rivera") mural (*[Man, Controller of the Universe](/wiki/Man,_Controller_of_the_Universe "Man, Controller of the Universe")*) depicts Trotsky with [Marx](/wiki/Marx "Marx") and [Engels](/wiki/Engels "Engels") as a true champion of the workers' struggle

In post-exile, Trotsky challenged claims from American socialist [Norman Thomas](/wiki/Norman_Thomas "Norman Thomas") that the Soviet Union would have been no better under his leadership. Trotsky countered that it was not a question of [personalities](/wiki/Personality "Personality") but opposing, [social interests](/wiki/Social_stratification "Social stratification") represented by the [bureaucracy](/wiki/Bureaucracy "Bureaucracy") and [the working class](/wiki/The_working_class "The working class").[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978434–435-237) More specifically, Trotsky attributed the retraction of the [progressive gains](/wiki/Soviet_Decree "Soviet Decree") of the October revolution, the mass purge and Soviet foreign policy as seen in [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") to the interests of the bureaucracy rather than the socialist proletariat or Bolshevik tradition.[[237]](#cite_note-238) He asserted that the excessive form of [totalitarianism](/wiki/Totalitarianism "Totalitarianism") under Stalinism would not have emerged under his variant of Bolshevism.[[238]](#cite_note-239)[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978434–436-240) Comparatively, he viewed his interpretation of Marxism to represent a [political](/wiki/Political "Political") and [moral](/wiki/Morality "Morality") regeneration of the Soviet Union against the Stalinist [bureaucracy](/wiki/Nomenklatura "Nomenklatura").[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978434–436-240)

Separately, Trotsky would defend his military decisions as necessary, making a comparison between his military endeavours with [Abraham Lincoln's](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") ruthlessness during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War").[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher20151482–1483-241) Deutscher drew attention to the fact that Trotsky preferred to exchange [hostages](/wiki/Hostages "Hostages") and [prisoners](/wiki/Prisoners "Prisoners") rather than execute them. He recounts that Trotsky had released [General Krasnov](/wiki/Pyotr_Krasnov "Pyotr Krasnov") on parole in 1918 after the [Kerensky–Krasnov uprising](/wiki/Kerensky%E2%80%93Krasnov_uprising "Kerensky–Krasnov uprising") during the initial stage of the Civil War, but the general would take up arms against the Soviets shortly afterwards.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015339–340-242)

Trotskyist [theoreticians](/wiki/Marxist_theory "Marxist theory") have disputed the view that the Stalinist dictatorship was a natural outgrowth of the Bolsheviks' actions as most of the original, central committee members from 1917 were later eliminated by Stalin.[[242]](#cite_note-243) [George Novack](/wiki/George_Novack "George Novack") stressed the initial efforts by the Bolsheviks to form a [multi-party](/wiki/Multi-party "Multi-party") government with the [Left Socialist Revolutionaries](/wiki/Left_Socialist_Revolutionaries "Left Socialist Revolutionaries") and bring other parties such as the [Mensheviks](/wiki/Mensheviks "Mensheviks") into political [legality](/wiki/Legality "Legality").[[243]](#cite_note-244) Upon the Menshevik
walkout from the Soviet congress, Trotsky released a number of arrested, socialist ministers of the [Provisional government](/wiki/Russian_Provisional_Government "Russian Provisional Government") from prison, at the request of [Julius Martov](/wiki/Julius_Martov "Julius Martov").[[244]](#cite_note-245) [Tony Cliff](/wiki/Tony_Cliff "Tony Cliff") argued the Bolshevik-Left Socialist Revolutionary coalition government dissolved the Constituent Assembly due to a number of reasons. They cited the outdated voter rolls, which did not acknowledge the split among the [Socialist Revolutionary](/wiki/Socialist_Revolutionary "Socialist Revolutionary") party and the assemblies' conflict with the [Congress of the Soviets](/wiki/All-Russian_Congress_of_Soviets "All-Russian Congress of Soviets") as an alternative democratic structure.[[245]](#cite_note-246)

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trotskyism&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Criticism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Permanent_Counter-Revolution_%28page_1_crop%29.jpg/250px-Permanent_Counter-Revolution_%28page_1_crop%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Permanent_Counter-Revolution_(page_1_crop).jpg)

Cover of a 1934 [Communist Party USA](/wiki/Communist_Party_USA "Communist Party USA") anti-Trotskyist pamphlet titled "Permanent Counter-Revolution"

Trotskyism has been criticised from various directions. In 1935, [Marxist–Leninist](/wiki/Marxist%E2%80%93Leninist "Marxist–Leninist") [Moissaye J. Olgin](/wiki/Moissaye_Joseph_Olgin "Moissaye Joseph Olgin") argued that Trotskyism was "the enemy of the working class" and "should be shunned by anybody who has sympathy for the revolutionary movement of the exploited and oppressed the world over."[[246]](#cite_note-247) The African American Marxist–Leninist [Harry Haywood](/wiki/Harry_Haywood "Harry Haywood"), who spent much time in the USSR during the 1920s and 1930s, stated that although he had been somewhat interested in Trotsky's ideas when he was young, he came to see it as "a disruptive force on the fringes of the international revolutionary movement" which eventually developed into "a counter-revolutionary conspiracy against the Party and the Soviet state". He continued to put forward his following belief:

> Trotsky was not defeated by bureaucratic decisions or Stalin's control of the Party apparatus—as his partisans and Trotskyite historians claim. He had his day in court and finally lost because his whole position flew in the face of Soviet and world realities. He was doomed to defeat because his ideas were incorrect and failed to conform to objective conditions, as well as the needs and interests of the Soviet people.[[247]](#cite_note-248)

Other figures associated with Marxism–Leninism criticized Trotskyist political theory, including [Régis Debray](/wiki/R%C3%A9gis_Debray "Régis Debray")[[248]](#cite_note-249) and [Earl Browder](/wiki/Earl_Browder "Earl Browder").[[249]](#cite_note-250)

In 1966, [Fidel Castro](/wiki/Fidel_Castro "Fidel Castro") said that "Even though at one time Trotskyism represented an erroneous position, but a position in the field of political ideas, Trotskyism became during the following years a vulgar instrument of imperialism and reaction."[[250]](#cite_note-251)

Polish philosopher [Leszek Kołakowski](/wiki/Leszek_Ko%C5%82akowski "Leszek Kołakowski") wrote: "Both Trotsky and Bukharin were emphatic in their assurances that forced labour was an organic part of the new society."[[251]](#cite_note-252)

Sociologist [Peter Beilharz](/wiki/Peter_Beilharz "Peter Beilharz") was critical of Trotskyism and viewed it as the most complete expression of [Jacobinism](/wiki/Jacobinism "Jacobinism"). Although, Beiharz acknowledged that Trotsky had opposed Jacobinism as a young democrat but in his view had extended the tradition during the period of [war communism](/wiki/War_communism "War communism") and through his ideological defence of terror.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeilharz19871–15-253) On the other hand, historian [Paul Le Blanc](/wiki/Paul_Le_Blanc_(historian) "Paul Le Blanc (historian)") found Beilharz's historical comparisons between Trotsky and earlier Jacobin figures to be unconvincing and suggested his analogies were more centred in rhetoric rather than in analysis due to his blurring of ideological concepts.[[253]](#cite_note-254)

Some [left communists](/wiki/Left_communists "Left communists"), such as [Paul Mattick](/wiki/Paul_Mattick "Paul Mattick"), claim that the October Revolution was totalitarian from the start. Therefore, Trotskyism has no fundamental differences from [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") in practice or theory.[[254]](#cite_note-255) French historian and Trotskyist [Pierre Broue](/wiki/Pierre_Broue "Pierre Broue") rejected this form of criticism and characterised it in the following way:

> "The theory according to which Stalin and Trotsky were two rival wild beasts is useful for historians serving those in power: the establishment of an equivalence between Stalinism and Trotskyism aids the idea of a continuity from Bolshevism and Leninism to Stalinism and strengthens a regime which fears revolutionary sentiments".[[255]](#cite_note-256)

British historian Christian Høgsbjerg believed that the academic literature and wider criticisms of Trotskyist organisations had minimised their historical role in building wider social movements. Høgsbjerg stressed the key role of British Trotskyists in various movements such as the [Vietnam Solidarity Campaign](/wiki/Vietnam_Solidarity_Campaign "Vietnam Solidarity Campaign") (1966–71), the [Anti-Nazi League](/wiki/Anti-Nazi_League "Anti-Nazi League") (1977–81), the [Anti Poll Tax](/wiki/All_Britain_Anti-Poll_Tax_Federation "All Britain Anti-Poll Tax Federation") Federation (1989–91) and the [Stop the War Coalition](/wiki/Stop_the_War_Coalition "Stop the War Coalition") (2001).[[256]](#cite_note-257)

In the United States, [Dwight Macdonald](/wiki/Dwight_Macdonald "Dwight Macdonald") broke with Trotsky and left the Trotskyist [Socialist Workers Party](/wiki/Socialist_Workers_Party_(United_States) "Socialist Workers Party (United States)") by raising the question of the [Kronstadt rebellion](/wiki/Kronstadt_rebellion "Kronstadt rebellion"), which Trotsky, as leader of the [Soviet Red Army](/wiki/Soviet_Red_Army "Soviet Red Army"), and the other Bolsheviks had brutally repressed. He then moved towards [democratic socialism](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism")[[257]](#cite_note-258) and [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism").[[258]](#cite_note-Politic-259)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The Lithuanian-American anarchist [Emma Goldman](/wiki/Emma_Goldman "Emma Goldman") raised a similar critique of Trotsky's role in the events around the Kronstadt rebellion. In her essay "Trotsky Protests Too Much", she says: "I admit, the dictatorship under Stalin's rule has become monstrous. That does not, however, lessen the guilt of Leon Trotsky as one of the actors in the revolutionary drama of which Kronstadt was one of the bloodiest scenes".[[259]](#cite_note-260)

Trotsky defended the actions of the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") in his essay "Hue and Cry over Kronstadt". He would also argue that the attitudes and social composition of the Kronstadt sailors had changed during the Civil War. Trotsky further argued that the isolated location of the naval fortress would have enabled financial funding to flow between Kronstadt and [White army](/wiki/White_army "White army") emigres.[[260]](#cite_note-261)[[261]](#cite_note-262) Separately, he would also argue that he and Lenin had intended to lift the ban on the [opposition parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_Soviet_Union "List of political parties in the Soviet Union") such as the [Mensheviks](/wiki/Mensheviks "Mensheviks") and [Socialist Revolutionaries](/wiki/Socialist_Revolutionaries "Socialist Revolutionaries") as soon as the economic and social conditions of [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Soviet_Russia "Soviet Russia") had improved after the Civil War.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015528-263)
